questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let $L_1$ be the line of intersection of the planes given by the equations

$2x + 3y + z = 4$ and $x + 2y + z = 5$.

Let $L_2$ be the line passing through the point $P(2, -1, 3)$ and parallel to $L_1$. Let $M$ denote the plane given by the equation

$2x + y - 2z = 6$.

Suppose that the line $L_2$ meets the plane $M$ at the point $Q$. Let $R$ be the foot of the perpendicular drawn from $P$ to the plane $M$.

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options: The length of the line segment $PQ$ is $9\sqrt{3}$
The length of the line segment $QR$ is $15$
The acute angle between the line segments $PQ$ and $PR$ is $\cos^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}}\right)$
questions.solution:
To find the line of intersection $ L_1 $ of the two planes given by the equations $ 2x + 3y + z = 4 $ and $ x + 2y + z = 5 $, we use these equations to obtain the line in standard form:

$ \begin{aligned} & L_1: 2x + 3y + z = 4 \\ & x + 2y + z = 5 \end{aligned} $

Solving these equations gives us the line $ L_1 $ in standard form:

$ \frac{x+7}{1} = \frac{y-6}{-1} = \frac{z}{1} $

For the line $ L_2 $ that passes through the point $ P(2, -1, 3) $ and is parallel to $ L_1 $, the equation is:

$ \frac{x-2}{1} = \frac{y+1}{-1} = \frac{z-3}{1} $

The equation of the plane $ M $ is:

$ 2x + y - 2z = 6 $

To find the coordinates of the point $ Q $ where $ L_2 $ meets the plane $ M $, assume the coordinates of $ Q $ are $ (\lambda+2, -\lambda-1, \lambda+3) $. Since $ Q $ lies on the plane $ M $, substituting these into the equation of the plane gives:

$ 2(\lambda+2) + (-\lambda-1) - 2(\lambda+3) = 6 $

Solving, we find:

$ \lambda = -9 $

Thus, the coordinates of $ Q $ are $ (-7, 8, -6) $.

For the foot of the perpendicular $ R(x_1, y_1, z_1) $ from $ P $ to the plane $ M $, using the formula for the distance from a point to a plane:

$ \frac{x_1-2}{2} = \frac{y_1+1}{1} = \frac{z_1-3}{-2} = \frac{-(4-1-6-6)}{9} $

The solution provides the coordinates of $ R $ as $ (4, 0, 1) $.

Calculating the line segments:

$ PQ = 9\sqrt{3} $ units

$ QR = \sqrt{234} $ units

$ PR = 3 $ units

For the acute angle $ \theta $ between $ PQ $ and $ PR $:

$ \cos \theta = \frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}} $

$ \sin \theta = \sqrt{\frac{26}{27}} $

The area of triangle $ \triangle PQR $ is given by:

$ \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \times PQ \times PR \times \sin \theta = \frac{3}{2} \sqrt{234} \text{ square units} $

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
The reaction sequence given below is carried out with 16 moles of X. The yield of the major product in each step is given below the product in parentheses. The amount (in grams) of S produced is ______.

Use: Atomic mass (in amu): H = 1, C = 12, O = 16, Br = 80
questions.solution:
$$ \text { Moles of } x=16 \mathrm{~mol} \text {. } $$
First step is Fitting reaction (Wurtz-Fittig reaction) Fitting reaction: Aryl halides when treated with sodium in dry ether, two. aryl groups joined to gether to form another aromatic compounds.
Here, 2 molecules of the reactant $X$ react with 2 Na in presence of dry ether and forms a coupling product with the elimination of 2 NaBr .
The reaction proceeds through a radical mechanism where the sodium metal abstracts a bromine atom. from the compound $x$, forming an aryl radical. These aryl radicals then combine to foem a biaryl (two acyl groups linked to gether)
$$ \text { Overall reaction can be written as, } $$
Second step is the hydrolysis reaction. The acetal hydrolysis occurs and forms - CHO group.
2 moles of $x$ reacts to form 1 mol $P$.
So, 1 mole of $X$ gives $\frac{1}{2}$ mol of $P$.
Given moles of $x$ is 16 mol.
So, the mole of $P=16 \mathrm{~mol} \times \frac{1}{2}$
$$ =8 \mathrm{~mol} $$
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Ratio between } x \text { and } P \text {, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & x: P \\ & 2 \mathrm{~mol}: 1 \mathrm{~mol} \\ & 1 \mathrm{~mol}: \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mol} \\ & 16 \mathrm{~mol}: 16 \times \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mol}=8 \mathrm{~mol} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$
$$ \begin{aligned} & P \xrightarrow[\text { 2) } \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}]{\text {1) } \mathrm{NaOH}, \Delta} Q \\ & (100 \%) \quad(50 \%) \end{aligned} $$
It is Cannizzaro reaction: One-CHO group converts to $-\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH}$ and the other - CHO to - COO .
Moles of $P \equiv 8 \mathrm{~mol}$
The reaction gives $50 \% Q$ from $100 \% P$.
So, the moles of $Q$ is half of moles of $P$.
Moles of $Q=\frac{8}{2}$
$=4 \mathrm{~mol}$
$$ Q \frac{1) \mathrm{NaOH}, \mathrm{CaO}}{2) \Delta} R $$
$$ (50 \%)\,\,\,\,\,\,(50 \%) $$
-COOH group is removed in this reaction. On heating with a mixture of NaOH and CaO (sodium hydroxide and calcium oxide), gives decarboxylation reaction. Cao acts as a drying agent and catalyst. NaOH is used to facilitate the removal of carbon dioxide $\left(\mathrm{CO}_2\right)$.
$$ \mathrm{NaOH}+ \mathrm{Cao} \text { mixture is known as soda lime. } $$
$$ \text { - } \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \text { group has no change during this reaction. } $$
$$ \text { Moles of } Q=4 \mathrm{~mol} $$
So, theoretically moles of $R$ is 4 mol $(1: 1$ ratio. between $Q$ and $R$ ).
But, the yield given is $50 \%$. so the moles of $R$ is half of moles of $Q$.
$$ \text { Moles of } \quad R=\frac{4}{2} $$
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \mathrm{~mol} $$
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{PBr}_3,\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5\right)_2 \mathrm{O}} \mathrm{~T} \\ & 50 \%\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \quad 50 \% \end{aligned} $$
When alcohol reacts with $\mathrm{PBr}_3$ in the presence of $\left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5\right)_2 \mathrm{O}$ (solvent), the reaction proceeds via $S_N 2$ mechanism and the hyphoxyl group is replaced by a bromine atom.
$$ \text { So, - } \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \text { group becomes - } \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{Br} \text {. } $$
Moles of $R=2 \mathrm{~mol}$.
The yield given is $50 \%$. So, the moles of is $T_{\text { }}$ half of moles of $R$.
Moles of $T=\frac{2}{2}$
$$ \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=1 \mathrm{~mol} $$
$$ \left(\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5\right)_2 \mathrm{O} \text { is the solvent } $$
reaction is known as Williamson ether synthesis. Ether compound is formed by reacting an alkoxide $\left(RO^-\right)^{}$with an alkyl halide $(R x)$. The reaction follows $S_{N 2}$ mechanism where the alkoxide acts as a nucleophile, attacking the alkylhalide's carbon atom and displacing the halide.
$$ \text { Formula: }\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5\right)_2\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_4\right)_2\left(\mathrm{CH}_2\right)_2 \mathrm{O} \mathrm{C}_{26} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}$$
Moles of T. $=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
Moles of s is half of moles of $T(50 \%$ yeld)
So, moles of $S=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~mol}$
$$ \text { Moles of } S \text { is determined. } $$
To calculate the amount, molecular weight must be known.
Molecular weight of $S \Rightarrow 26 \times 12 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}+22 \times 1 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}+1 \times$ $16 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
$$ =312+22+16=350 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol} $$
$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Moles } & =\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { molarmass }} \\ \text { So, mass } & =\text { moles } x \text { molar mass } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \text { mot } \times 350 \text { g/mol } \\ & =175 . \mathrm{g} \end{aligned} $$
$$ \text { Answer: } 175 $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: Identify the compound that undergoes self-aldol condensation in the presence of cold dilute alkali, forming a $\beta$-hydroxy aldehyde:
questions.solution:
Aldol condensation requires the presence of at least one $\alpha$-hydrogen in the aldehyde. Among the options, acetaldehyde $\left(\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}\right)$ has $\alpha$-hydrogens and undergoes the reaction.

$$ 2 \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}+\text { dilute } \mathrm{NaOH}\longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \text { (3-hydroxy butanal) } $$

This $\beta$-hydroxy aldehyde may further dehydrate to form an $\alpha, \beta$-unsaturated aldehyde:

$$ \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OH}) \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{CHO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CHCHO}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$

The other compounds either lack $\alpha$-hydrogens or are deactivated (e.g., benzaldehyde, phenylacetylene derivative).

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A pure resistive circuit element $X$, when connected to an AC supply of peak voltage 200 V , gives a peak current of 5 A . A second circuit element $Y$, when connected to the same AC supply also gives the same value of peak current but the current lags behind by $90^{\circ}$. If the series combination of $X$ and $Y$ is connected to the same supply, what will be the rms value of current?
questions.solution:
Clearly first element is resistance and second element is inductor

$$ \begin{aligned} R & =X_L=\frac{200}{5}=40 \Omega \\ \therefore Z & =\sqrt{R_f^2+X_L^2}=40 \sqrt{2} \Omega \\ I_{\mathrm{rms}} & =\frac{V_{\mathrm{rms}}}{Z}=\frac{200 / \sqrt{2}}{40 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: A voltage $v=v_0 \sin \omega t$ applied to a circuit drives a current $i=i_0 \sin (\omega t+\phi)$ in the circuit. The average power consumed in the circuit over a cycle is
questions.solution:
$v=v_0 \sin \omega t$ and $i=i_0 \sin (\omega t+\phi)$

Average power over a full cycle is

$$ \begin{aligned} P_{a v} & =v_{\mathrm{rms}} i_{\mathrm{rms}} \cos \phi \\ & =\frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}} \frac{i_0}{\sqrt{2}} \cos \phi \\ \therefore \quad P_{a v} & =\frac{v_0 i_0}{2} \cos \phi \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
A circuit with an electrical load having impedance $Z$ is connected with an AC source as shown in the diagram. The source voltage varies in time as $V(t)=300 \sin (400 t) \mathrm{V}$, where $t$ is time in s . List-I shows various options for the load. The possible currents $i(t)$ in the circuit as a function of time are given in List-II.

Choose the option that describes the correct match between the entries in List-I to those in ListII.

List–I List–II
(P) (1)
(Q) (2)
(R) (3)
(S) (4)
(5)
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow i_0=\frac{v_0}{R}=\frac{300}{30}=10 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \text { and } \phi=0 \\ & \begin{array}{l} So (P) \rightarrow 3 \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} z & =\sqrt{R^2+(\omega L)^2} \quad\left(\text { as } x_L=\omega L\right) \\ & =\sqrt{(30)^2+\left(400 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{900+1600}=\sqrt{2500} \\ z=50 \Omega & \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { So } & i_0=\frac{V_0}{2}=\frac{300}{50}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ \phi & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x_L}{R}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega L}{R}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{400 \times 106 \times 10^{-3}}{36}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \phi & =53^{\circ}(l \mathrm{ag}) \\ & \therefore Q \rightarrow 5 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} z & =\sqrt{R^2+\left(x_C-x_L\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{R^2+\left(\frac{1}{\omega c}-\omega L\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(30)^2+\left(\frac{1}{400 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}-400 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2} \\ z & =\sqrt{900+\left(\frac{1}{20 \times 10^{-3}}-10\right)^2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{r} z=\sqrt{900+(50-10)^2}=\sqrt{900+1600} \\ z=\sqrt{2500}=50 \Omega \\ i_0=\frac{v_0}{2}=\frac{300 }{50}=6 \mathrm{~A} \\ \phi=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{x_L-x_C}{R}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{10-50}{30}\right] \\ =\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{40 }{30}\right|=53^{\circ} \text { (Lead) } \end{array}\\ &(R) \rightarrow 2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} z & =\sqrt{(60)^2+\left(\frac{1}{400 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}-400 \times 125 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{(60)^2+(50-50)^2} \\ z & =60 \Omega \\ & i_0=\frac{300}{60}=5 \mathrm{~A} \\ \phi & =\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{x_L-x_C}{R}\right|=\tan ^{-1}\left|\frac{0}{60}\right|=0^{\circ} \end{aligned} $$

So $(s) \rightarrow 1$

Hence, option (A) is correct.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: In a certain code language, 'SPAM' is coded as ' 36 ' and 'JUG' is coded as ' 27 '. How will 'FROCK' be coded in that language?
questions.solution:
As, SPAM $=36$

$$ \begin{gathered} 4 \times 9=36 \\ 4=\text { Number of letters } \end{gathered} $$

And JUG = 27

Number of letters $=3$

$$ 3 \times 9=27 $$

Similarly, $\mathrm{FROCK}=45$

Number of letters $=5$

$$ 5 \times 9=45 $$

Hence, option (b) is the correct answer.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Directions :In the following items, a pair of words is provided. You are required to select the option that most appropriately describes the meaning of both the words and mark your response on the Answer Sheet accordingly  

Seldom and Often
options: Seldom means hardly ever and often means time and again
Seldom means time and again and often means hardly ever
Seldom means never and often means not at all
Seldom means frequently and often means time and again
questions.solution:
The correct option is Option 1.

Step by step:

  • Seldom means hardly ever or rarely

  • Often means frequently or time and again

So,

  • Option 1 is correct:

    Seldom means hardly ever and often means time and again

Answer: Option 1

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The function $f(x)=\frac{x}{\sin x}$ is strictly increasing in the interval.
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { We have, } f(x)=\frac{x}{\sin x} \\ & \therefore f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\sin x-x \cos x}{\sin ^2 x}=\frac{g(x)}{\sin ^2 x} \text {, where } g(x) \\ & =\sin x-x \cos x \\ & \text { Now, } g(x)=\sin x-x \cos x \\ & g^{\prime}(x)=\cos x-\cos x+x \sin x=x \sin x \\ & \Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x)>0 \text { for all } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \qquad\left[\because \sin x>0 \text { for } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow g(x)$ is an increasing function on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow g(x)>g(0), \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow g(x)>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{g(x)}{\sin ^2 x}>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \quad \quad\left[\because \sin ^2 x>0 \text { for } x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right] \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0, \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $f(x)$ is strictly increasing on $\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: For what values of the parameter ' $a$ ' does the function $f(x)=x^3+3(a-7) x^2+3\left(a^2-9\right) x-1$ have a positive point of maximum.
questions.solution:
We have,

$$ \begin{aligned} f(x) & =x^3+3(a-7) x^2+3\left(a^2-9\right) x-1 \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =3 x^2+6(a-7) x+3\left(a^2-9\right) \\ & =3\left[x^2+2(a-7) x+\left(a^2-9\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$

For local maximum, we must have $f^{\prime}(x)=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x^2+2(a-7) x+a^2-9=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-(a-7) \pm \sqrt{58-14 a} \end{aligned} $$

Let $x_1=-(a-7)+\sqrt{58-14 a}$ and

$$ x_2=-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a} $$

For $x_1, x_2$ to be real, we must have

$$ 58-14 a>0 \Rightarrow a<\frac{58}{14}=\frac{29}{7} $$

Now, $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=3[2 x+2(a-7)]=6[x+a-7]$

Clearly, $f^{\prime \prime}\left(x_2\right)=6\left(x_2+a-7\right)=-6 \sqrt{58-14 a}<0$

So, $x_2=-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a}$ is a point of local maximum.

Now, $x_2>0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow-(a-7)-\sqrt{58-14 a}>0 \\ & \Rightarrow 7-a>\sqrt{58-14 a} \Rightarrow(7-a)^2>58-14 a \\ & \Rightarrow a^2-9>0 \Rightarrow a<-3 \text { or } a>3 \\ & \text { Hence, } a \in(-\infty,-3) \cup\left(3, \frac{29}{7}\right) \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The slope of the curve $2 y^2=a x^2+b$ at $(1,-1)$ is -1 . Then, the value of $b$ is
questions.solution:
$$ \text { The equation of the curve is } 2 y^2=a x^2+b \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $x$, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 4 y \frac{d y}{d x}=2 a x \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{a x}{2 y} \\ \therefore \quad & \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(1,-1)}=\frac{-a}{2} \end{aligned} $$

Also, since the slope of the tangent at $(1,-1)$ is -1 .

$$ \therefore \quad \frac{-a}{2}=-1 \Rightarrow a=2 $$

Since, the point $(1,-1)$ lies on Eq. (i)

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2(-1)^2=a(1)^2+b=a+b=2 \\ \Rightarrow & 2+b=2 \Rightarrow b=0 \end{array} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let ℝ denote the set of all real numbers. Let f: ℝ → ℝ be defined by

$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{6x + \sin x}{2x + \sin x}, & \text{if } x \neq 0, \\ \dfrac{7}{3}, & \text{if } x = 0. \end{cases}$

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options: The point $x = 0$ is a point of local maxima of $f$
The point $x = 0$ is a point of local minima of $f$
Number of points of local maxima of $f$ in the interval $[\pi, 6\pi]$ is 3
Number of points of local minima of $f$ in the interval $[2\pi, 4\pi]$ is 1
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=3 \\ & \text { since } 3>\frac{7}{3} \Rightarrow 3>f(0) \end{aligned} $$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=0$ is local minima ….option (B) is correct

Now,

$$ \begin{aligned} & f(x)=\frac{6 x+\sin x}{2 x+\sin x}=1+\frac{4 x}{2 x+\sin x} \\ & f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{4[(2 x+\sin x) \cdot 1-x(2+\cos x)]}{(2 x+\sin x)^2}=\frac{4(\sin x-x \cos x)}{(2 x+\sin x)^2} \\ & =\frac{4 \cos x(\tan x-x)}{(2 x+\sin x)^2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Options (C) & (D) are also correct. } $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is ∠A equal to if the area of the triangle is maximum?
options: 5π/12
questions.solution:
1. Let AB = x, so AC = 3 – x.

  2. Since ∠B = 90°, by Pythagoras

    $$BC = \sqrt{AC^2 - AB^2} = \sqrt{(3 - x)^2 - x^2} = \sqrt{9 - 6x}\,. $$

  3. Area $A$ of ΔABC:

    $$A = \tfrac12 \times AB \times BC = \tfrac12\,x\,\sqrt{9 - 6x}\,. $$

  4. To maximise $A$, set $\dfrac{dA}{dx}=0$:

    $$A = \tfrac12\,x\,(9 - 6x)^{1/2},$$

    $$\frac{dA}{dx} = \frac12\,(9-6x)^{1/2} - \frac{6x}{4\,(9-6x)^{1/2}} = \frac{18 - 18x}{4\sqrt{9-6x}} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x = 1.$$

  5. Thus

    $$AB = 1,\quad AC = 2,\quad BC = \sqrt{9 - 6\cdot1} = \sqrt3.$$

  6. ∠A satisfies

    $$\tan A = \frac{BC}{AB} = \frac{\sqrt3}{1} = \sqrt3 \;\Longrightarrow\; A = \frac{\pi}{3}.$$

Answer: Option C, $\displaystyle \pi/3$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the maximum area of the triangle?
questions.solution:
1. Let AB = x, BC = y. Then AC (hypotenuse) = $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$.

  2. Given

    $$x + \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = 3\,. $$

  3. From this,

    $$\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = 3 - x \quad\Rightarrow\quad x^2 + y^2 = (3 - x)^2 = 9 - 6x + x^2$$

    $$\therefore y^2 = 9 - 6x\,,\quad y = \sqrt{9 - 6x}\,. $$

  4. Area of $\triangle ABC$

    $$A = \frac12\,(AB)(BC) = \frac12\,x\,y = \frac12\,x\sqrt{9 - 6x}\,. $$

  5. To find the maximum, set

    $$f(x) = x\sqrt{9 - 6x}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f^2(x) = x^2(9 - 6x) = 9x^2 - 6x^3\,. $$

    Differentiate:

    $$\frac{d}{dx}\bigl(f^2(x)\bigr) = 18x - 18x^2 = 18x(1 - x)\,. $$

    Setting $18x(1 - x)=0$ gives $x=0$ or $x=1$. Discard $x=0$.

  6. For $x=1$,

    $$y = \sqrt{9 - 6\cdot1} = \sqrt{3},\qquad A_{\max} = \frac12\cdot1\cdot\sqrt{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}2\,. $$

Answer: Option A.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the nature of the curve?
options: A parabola with vertex at origin and focus at (2,0)
A parabola with vertex at origin and focus at (1, 0)
questions.solution:
1. Let slope of tangent be

    $ \frac{dy}{dx}=4. $

  2. Integrate both sides w.r.t. $x$:

    $ y=\int 4\,dx=4x+C. $

  3. Curve passes through origin $(0,0)$:

    $ 0=4\cdot0+C\quad\implies\quad C=0. $

  4. Hence

    $ y=4x, $

    a straight line of slope 4. At $x=1$, $y=4\cdot1=4$, so it passes through $(1,4)$.

Answer: Option A.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
I. f(x) is an increasing function.
II. f(x) has local maximum at x = 0
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.solution:
Understanding the Function $f(x) = x^2 + 9$

This function represents a parabola that opens upwards. You might remember from your studies that for a quadratic function $ax^2 + bx + c$, if $a > 0$, the parabola opens upwards and has a minimum value. If $a < 0$, it opens downwards and has a maximum value. Here, for $f(x) = x^2 + 9$, $a=1$, which is positive, so it's an upward-opening parabola.

Let's find its vertex. The $x$-coordinate of the vertex of a parabola $ax^2 + bx + c$ is given by $x = -b/(2a)$.

For $f(x) = x^2 + 9$, we can write it as $x^2 + 0x + 9$. So, $a=1$ and $b=0$.

The $x$-coordinate of the vertex is $x = -0/(2 \times 1) = 0$.

At $x=0$, the value of the function is $f(0) = 0^2 + 9 = 9$.

So, the vertex is at $(0, 9)$. Since the parabola opens upwards, this vertex represents the lowest point, which means it's a minimum.

Statement I: $f(x)$ is an increasing function.

For a function to be an increasing function, its value must continuously increase as $x$ increases over its entire domain. Mathematically, this means its derivative, $f'(x)$, must be greater than zero for all $x$.

Let's find the derivative of $f(x)$:

$$f(x) = x^2 + 9$$

Using the rules of differentiation:

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(x^2) + \frac{d}{dx}(9)$$

$$f'(x) = 2x + 0$$

$$f'(x) = 2x$$

Now, let's check for what values of $x$ is $f'(x) > 0$:

$$2x > 0 \implies x > 0$$

This tells us that $f(x)$ is increasing only when $x > 0$.

What happens when $x < 0$?

If $x < 0$, then $2x < 0$. For example, if $x = -1$, $f'(-1) = 2(-1) = -2$, which is less than 0. This means the function is decreasing for $x < 0$.

Since the function is decreasing for $x < 0$ and increasing only for $x > 0$, it is not an increasing function over its entire domain (all real numbers).

Therefore, Statement I is incorrect.

Statement II: $f(x)$ has local maximum at $x = 0$.

To find local maxima or minima, we first find the critical points by setting the first derivative to zero:

$$f'(x) = 0$$

$$2x = 0$$

$$x = 0$$

So, $x=0$ is a critical point. Now, we need to determine if it's a local maximum or a local minimum. We can use the second derivative test.

First, let's find the second derivative:

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(f'(x)) = \frac{d}{dx}(2x)$$

$$f''(x) = 2$$

Now, evaluate the second derivative at the critical point $x=0$:

$$f''(0) = 2$$

Since $f''(0) = 2$, which is greater than 0 ($f''(x) > 0$), the function has a local minimum at $x=0$.

As we discussed at the beginning, $f(x) = x^2 + 9$ is an upward-opening parabola, and its vertex at $x=0$ is indeed a minimum point. It does not have a local maximum at $x=0$.

Therefore, Statement II is incorrect.

Conclusion

Since both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect, the correct option is the one that says neither statement is correct.

The final answer is $\boxed{\text{Neither I nor II}}$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is the slope of the tangent to the curve y = f(x) at x = 0.5
questions.solution:
Consider the function

$ y = f(x) = x \cdot \frac{\sin^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1 - x^2}} + \ln \sqrt{1 - x^2} $

Now differentiate $ f(x) $ step by step:

$ f'(x) = \left( \sin^{-1}x + \frac{x}{\sqrt{1 - x^2}} \right) \cdot \sqrt{1 - x^2} + x \cdot \sin^{-1}x + \frac{2x}{2\sqrt{1 - x^2}} $

$ + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - x^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 - x^2}} \cdot (-2x) $

After simplification,

$ = \sqrt{1 - x^2} \cdot \sin^{-1}x + x + \frac{x^2 \cdot \sin^{-1}x}{\sqrt{1 - x^2}} - \frac{x}{1 - x^2} $

Combining the terms gives

$ f'(x) = \frac{(1 - x^2)\sin^{-1}x + x\sqrt{1 - x^2} + x^2 \sin^{-1}x - x\sqrt{1 - x^2}}{(1 - x^2)\sqrt{1 - x^2}} $

$ = \frac{\sin^{-1}x}{(1 - x^2)\sqrt{1 - x^2}} $

Evaluating this derivative at $ x = 0.5 $:

$ f'(0.5) = \frac{\sin^{-1}(0.5)}{(1 - 0.25)\sqrt{1 - 0.25}} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{6}}{\frac{3}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{\pi}{6} \cdot \frac{8}{3\sqrt{3}} = \frac{4\pi\sqrt{3}}{27} $

Therefore, the slope of $ y = f(x) $ at $ x = 0.5 $ is $\frac{4\pi\sqrt{3}}{27}$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: The function has
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=1-\sqrt[3]{(x-1)^2}=1-(x-1)^{2/3} $$

We have to check the nature of the function at $x=1$.

First, find the value of the function at $x=1$:

$$ f(1)=1-(1-1)^{2/3}=1-0=1 $$

Now observe that for every real $x$,

$$ (x-1)^2 \ge 0 $$

So,

$$ \sqrt[3]{(x-1)^2} \ge 0 $$

Therefore,

$$ f(x)=1-\sqrt[3]{(x-1)^2} \le 1 $$

This means for all $x$,

$$ f(x)\le f(1) $$

Hence, the value at $x=1$ is greater than or equal to the value at every nearby point as well as every real point.

So, $f(x)$ has a maximum at $x=1$.

Therefore, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 2: a maximum at } x=1} $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question:
A wire of length 20 cm is to be bent into a rectangle. Which of the following statements is/are correct?

I. The rectangle of the largest area is the square.

II. It is possible to form a rectangle of an area of$ 27c m ^ 2$

Select the answer using the code given below.
questions.solution:
Let the length and breadth of the rectangle be $l$ cm and $b$ cm.

Since the wire of length $20$ cm is bent into a rectangle, its perimeter is $20$ cm.

So,

$$ 2(l+b)=20 $$

$$ l+b=10 $$

Now, area of the rectangle is

$$ A=lb $$

Substitute $b=10-l$:

$$ A=l(10-l) $$

$$ A=10l-l^2 $$

This is a quadratic expression. To find its maximum value, we complete the square:

$$ A=-(l^2-10l) $$

$$ A=-\left(l^2-10l+25-25\right) $$

$$ A=-\left((l-5)^2-25\right) $$

$$ A=25-(l-5)^2 $$

Since $(l-5)^2 \ge 0$, the maximum value of $A$ is

$$ A=25 $$

and this happens when

$$ l-5=0 \Rightarrow l=5 $$

Then,

$$ b=10-5=5 $$

So the rectangle is a square.

Hence, Statement I is correct.

Now check Statement II:

The maximum possible area is

$$ 25 \text{ cm}^2 $$

So an area of

$$ 27 \text{ cm}^2 $$

is not possible.

Hence, Statement II is false.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1: I only}} $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the function f(x) = 1/(x - 5)

Statement-I: f(x) is decreasing on the intervals x < 5 and x > 5

Statement-II: f''(x) > 0 for all x ≠ 5.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct but Statement-II does not explain Statement-I
Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is not correct
Statement-I is not correct but Statement-ll is correct
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=\frac{1}{x-5}=(x-5)^{-1}, \qquad x\neq 5 $$

We will check both statements one by one.

Checking Statement-I

To test whether the function is increasing or decreasing, we find the first derivative.

$$ f'(x)=\frac{d}{dx}(x-5)^{-1} = -1(x-5)^{-2} = -\frac{1}{(x-5)^2} $$

Now, for every $x\neq 5$,

$$ (x-5)^2>0 $$

So,

$$ f'(x)=-\frac{1}{(x-5)^2}<0 $$

Since $f'(x)<0$ for all $x\neq 5$, the function is decreasing on each interval of its domain:

  • for $x<5$

  • for $x>5$

So, Statement-I is correct.

Checking Statement-II

Now find the second derivative.

$$ f'(x)=-(x-5)^{-2} $$

Differentiating again,

$$ f''(x)= -(-2)(x-5)^{-3} = 2(x-5)^{-3} = \frac{2}{(x-5)^3} $$

Now observe:

  • if $x>5$, then $(x-5)^3>0$, so

    $$ f''(x)>0 $$

  • if $x<5$, then $(x-5)^3<0$, so

    $$ f''(x)<0 $$

Therefore, $f''(x)>0$ is not true for all $x\neq 5$.

So, Statement-II is not correct.

Final conclusion

  • Statement-I is correct

  • Statement-II is not correct

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 3}} $$

That is, Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is not correct.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements:

Statement-I: The function  $f(x)=\frac{x^{3}+128}{x}$ has a minimum value 48 at x = 4

Statement-II: As x increases through 4, f'(x) changes sign from positive to negative.

Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
options: Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I
Both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct but Statement-II does not explain Statement-I
Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is not correct
Statement-I is not correct but Statement-II is correct
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=\frac{x^3+128}{x}=x^2+\frac{128}{x}, \qquad x\ne 0 $$

We will check both statements.

First, find the derivative:

$$ f'(x)=\frac{d}{dx}\left(x^2+\frac{128}{x}\right) =2x-\frac{128}{x^2} $$

Now check at $x=4$:

$$ f(4)=4^2+\frac{128}{4}=16+32=48 $$

So at $x=4$, the function value is indeed $48$.

Now solve $f'(x)=0$:

$$ 2x-\frac{128}{x^2}=0 $$

$$ 2x=\frac{128}{x^2} $$

$$ 2x^3=128 $$

$$ x^3=64 $$

$$ x=4 $$

So $x=4$ is a critical point.

Now check the sign of $f'(x)$ around $x=4$.

  • For $x<4$, take $x=3$:

$$ f'(3)=2(3)-\frac{128}{9}=6-\frac{128}{9}<0 $$

  • For $x>4$, take $x=5$:

$$ f'(5)=10-\frac{128}{25}>0 $$

So, as $x$ increases through $4$, $f'(x)$ changes from negative to positive, not from positive to negative.

This means:

  • $f(x)$ has a minimum at $x=4$

  • minimum value is $48$

Therefore, Statement-I is correct.

But Statement-II is not correct.

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 3}} $$

Statement-I is correct but Statement-II is not correct.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let ℝ denote the set of all real numbers. Then the area of the region

$ \left\{ (x, y) \in \mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R} : x > 0, y > \frac{1}{x}, 5x - 4y - 1 > 0, 4x + 4y - 17 < 0 \right\} $

is
questions.solution:
$$ \text { Area }=\underset{\substack{\downarrow \\ \text { Trapezium APBC }}}{\frac{1}{2}\left(1+\frac{9}{4}\right)}+\underset{\substack{\downarrow \\ \text { Trapezium PDCE }}}{\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{9}{4}+\frac{1}{4}\right)} \cdot 2-\int_1^4 \frac{\mathrm{dx}}{\mathrm{x}} $$

$$ \text { Area }=\frac{13}{8}+\frac{20}{8}-\log _e 4=\left(\frac{33}{8}-\log _e 4\right) $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: What is the area enclosed by the curves $y=x^4$ and $y=x^{\frac{1}{3}}$
questions.solution:
Here, $x^4=x^{\frac{1}{3}} \Rightarrow x^{12}=x$

$$ \Rightarrow x\left(x^{11}-1\right)=0 $$

So, $x=0$ or $x^{11}=1$ which gives $x=1$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad A & =\int_0^1\left(x^{\frac{1}{3}}-x^4\right) d x=\left[\frac{3}{4} x^{\frac{4}{3}}-\frac{1}{5} x^5\right]_0^1 \\ & =\left(\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{5}\right)=\frac{15-4}{20}=\frac{11}{20} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: . What is the area bounded by the curve, the x-axis and the line x = 4?
questions.solution:
1. The question tells us that the slope of the curve everywhere is 4. This means the derivative, or rate of change, of the function is 4.

    So, $ f'(x) = 4 $

    To find the original function, we find the antiderivative:

    $$f(x)=\int 4\,dx=4x + C$$

  2. We also know the curve goes through the point (0, 0). That means when $ x = 0 $, $ y = 0 $.

    Plugging in, $$0 = f(0) = 4\cdot 0 + C \implies C = 0$$

    So the function is simply:

    $$y = f(x) = 4x$$

  3. We want the area between the curve $ y = 4x $, the x-axis, and the lines $ x = 0 $ and $ x = 4 $.

    To find this area, set up the definite integral:

    $$\text{Area} = \int_{0}^{4} y\,dx = \int_{0}^{4} 4x\,dx$$

  4. Now let's solve the integral:

    $$\int 4x\,dx = 2x^2$$
    So, $$\text{Area} = [2x^2]_{0}^{4} = 2\cdot4^2 - 2\cdot0^2 = 2\cdot16 = 32$$

Answer: 32 square units (Option C).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the area bounded by the curve f(x) and y = 3?
questions.solution:
The graphs of

$$y=f(x)=|x-3|\quad\text{and}\quad y=3$$

intersect where

$$|x-3|=3\;\Rightarrow\;x-3=\pm3\;\Rightarrow\;x=0\text{ or }6.$$

So the bounded region is the “inverted-V” below the line $y=3$ and above the vertex at $(3,0)$, with endpoints $(0,3)$ and $(6,3)$.

• These three points $(0,3),\,(3,0),\,(6,3)$ form a triangle.

• Base of the triangle lies along $y=3$ from $x=0$ to $x=6$, so

$$\text{base}=6-0=6\text{ units.}$$

• Height is the vertical distance from the line $y=3$ down to the vertex $y=0$, so

$$\text{height}=3-0=3\text{ units.}$$

Area of a triangle is $\tfrac12\times\text{base}\times\text{height}$:

$$\text{Area}=\frac12\times6\times3=9\text{ square units.}$$

Answer: Option D (9 square units).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the area bounded by the function f(x) and the x-axis?
questions.solution:
The curve cuts the x–axis where

$$x^2-1=0\;\Longrightarrow\;x=\pm1.$$

On the interval $[-1,1]$, $f(x)=x^2-1\le0$, so the required area is

$ \int_{-1}^{1}\bigl|x^2-1\bigr|\,dx =\int_{-1}^{1}(1-x^2)\,dx. $

Step-by-step:

Write the integral:

$$A=\int_{-1}^{1}(1-x^2)\,dx.$$

Find an antiderivative:

$$\int(1-x^2)\,dx = x - \frac{x^3}{3} + C.$$

Evaluate from $-1$ to $1$:

$ A=\Bigl[x - \tfrac{x^3}{3}\Bigr]_{-1}^{1} =\Bigl(1-\tfrac{1}{3}\Bigr)-\Bigl(-1+\tfrac{1}{3}\Bigr) =\tfrac{2}{3}-\bigl(-\tfrac{2}{3}\bigr) =\tfrac{4}{3}. $

Hence the area is $\displaystyle \tfrac{4}{3}$ square units.

Answer: Option C.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is the area bounded by the curve f(x) the x-axis and the lines x = - 2 and x = 1
questions.solution:
The required area is:

$ \left| \int_{-2}^{0} x^2 \, dx \right| + \int_{0}^{1} x^2 \, dx $

$ = -\left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{-2}^{0} + \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{1} $

$ = \left[ 0 + \frac{8}{3} \right] + \left[ \frac{1}{3} - 0 \right] = \frac{8}{3} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{9}{3} = 3 $

Therefore, the correct answer is Option 4.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The degeneracy of a hydrogen atom whose energy equals $-R_{\mathrm{H}}$ / 16
questions.solution:
The energy of an electron in a hydrogen atom is given by the formula:

$$E_n = -\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2}$$

We are told that the energy of the atom is equal to $$-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}$$. To find the principal quantum number $ n $, we compare the two expressions:

$$-\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{n^2} = -\frac{R_{\mathrm{H}}}{16}$$

Canceling out $ -R_{\mathrm{H}} $ from both sides gives:

$$\frac{1}{n^2} = \frac{1}{16}$$

Therefore, $ n^2 = 16 \Rightarrow n = 4 $.

In a hydrogen atom, all orbitals that share the same principal quantum number $ n $ have the same energy. This means they are called degenerate. The total number of these degenerate orbitals is given by $ n^2 $.

So, for $ n = 4 $:

Degeneracy $ = n^2 = 4^2 = 16 $.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: If the de-Broglie wavelength of a particle is equal to $10 \sqrt{h / m}$, then what is the ratio of its speed to mass?
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength is given by the formula:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{mV}$$

According to the question, the wavelength is also given as:

$$\lambda = 10 \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Now, equating the two expressions for $ \lambda $:

$$\frac{h}{mV} = 10 \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Multiply both sides by $ mV $:

$$h = 10 \, mV \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Divide both sides by 10:

$$\frac{h}{10} = mV \sqrt{\frac{h}{m}}$$

Square both sides to remove the square root:

$$\left(\frac{h}{10}\right)^2 = (mV)^2 \times \frac{h}{m}$$

Simplify the equation:

$$\frac{h^2}{100} = m^2V^2 \frac{h}{m}$$

$$\frac{h}{100} = mV^2$$

Now, solve for $ V $:

$$V = \sqrt{\frac{h}{100m}}$$

To find the ratio of speed to mass, $ \frac{V}{m} $:

$$\frac{V}{m} = \frac{1}{m} \sqrt{\frac{h}{100m}} = \frac{\sqrt{h}}{10 \, m^{3/2}}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: Consider a hydrogen atom with its electron in the $n$th orbit. An electromagnetic radiation of wavelength 90 nm is used to ionize the atom. If the kinetic energy of the ejected electron is 10.4 eV , then the value of $n$ is ( $h c=1242 \mathrm{eV} \mathrm{nm}$ )
questions.solution:
Kinetic energy of ejected electron $=$ Energy of incident photon - Energy required to ionize the electron from $n$th orbit (all in eV )

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 10.4=\frac{1242}{90}-\left|E_n\right| \\ & 10.4=13.8-\frac{13.6}{n^2} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{13.6}{n^2}=3.4 \\ & \left(\text { as } E_n \propto \frac{1}{n^2} \text { and } E_1=-13.6 \mathrm{eV}\right) \end{array} $$

Solving this equation, we get

               $$ n=2 $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The mass of proton is 1.0073 u and that of neutron is $1.0087 \mathrm{u}(\mathrm{u}=$ atomic mass unit). The binding energy of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$ is
questions.solution:
The nucleus of ${ }_2 \mathrm{He}^4$ contains 2 neutrons and 2 protons.

Mass of 2 protons $=2 \times 1.0073=2.0146 u$

Mass of 2 neutrons $=2 \times 1.0087=2.0174 u$

Total mass of 2 protons and 2 neutrons

$$ =(2.0146+2.0174)=4.032 u $$

Mass of helium nucleus $=4.0015 u$

Thus, mass defect is lacking of mass in forming the helium nucleus from 2 protons and 2 neutrons.

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \Delta m & =\text { Mass defect } \\ & =(4.032-4.0015) \mathrm{u}=0.0305 \mathrm{u} \\ & =0.0305 \mathrm{amu} \end{aligned} $$

but $1 \mathrm{amu}=931 \mathrm{MeV}$

Hence, binding energy,

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E & =(\Delta m) \times 931 \\ & =0.0305 \times 931=28.4 \mathrm{MeV} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
Assertion (A) : The potential energy of an electon revolving in any stationary orbit in a hydrogen atom is positive.

Reason (R) : The total energy of a charged particle is always positive.
options: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason $(R)$ is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
Potential Energy of an Electron

In a hydrogen atom, the potential energy ($U$) of an electron is given by:

$$ U=-\frac{k e^2}{r} $$

The negative sign means that the potential energy is less than zero (negative), not positive.

This shows Assertion (A) is incorrect because it says the potential energy is positive, but in reality, it is negative.

Total Energy of a Charged Particle

The total energy ($E$) of an electron in a hydrogen atom is:

$$E=-\frac{k e^2}{2 r}$$

This energy is also negative. This means the electron is held by the nucleus, and not free.

So, Reason (R) is also incorrect because total energy is not always positive. In atoms like hydrogen, it is negative.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: Which of the following figures correctly represents the shape of curve of binding energy per nucleon as a function of mass number?
questions.solution: The curve of binding energy per nucleon as a function of mass number shows a peak around iron ( Fe , mass number 56). This is because the binding energy per nucleon increases with mass number up to iron and then decreases for heavier elements.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: Prove that, in Bohr model of hydrogen atom, the time period of revolution of an electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit is proportional to $n^3$.
questions.solution:
Using Bohr's theory

Centripetal force $=$ Electrostatic force

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{m v^2}{r} & =\frac{k Z e^2}{r^2} \\\Rightarrow r & =\frac{k Z e^2}{m v^2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

Angular momentum is quantised, i.e.,

$$ \begin{aligned} m v r & =\frac{n h}{2 \pi} \\ \Rightarrow \quad r & =\frac{n h}{2 \pi m v} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Equating (i) and (ii)

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{n h}{2 \pi m v} & =\frac{k Z e^2}{m v^2} \\ v & =\frac{2 \pi k Z e^2}{n h} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

Putting Eq. (iii) in (ii)

$$ \begin{aligned} & r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{2 \pi m \times 2 \pi k Z e^2} \\ & r=\frac{n^2 h^2}{4 \pi^2 m k Z e^2} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iv)\\ & \omega=\frac{v}{r} \\ & T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=\frac{2 \pi r}{v}=\frac{n^3 h^3}{4 \pi^2 m k^2 Z^2 e^4} \end{aligned} $$

(Putting the values of $v$ and $r$ from (iii) and (iv))

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) Define 'Mass defect' and 'Binding energy' of a nucleus. Describe 'Fission process' on the basis of binding energy per nucleon.

(b) A deuteron contains a proton and a neutron and has a mass of 2.013553 u . Calculate the mass defect for it in $u$ and its energy equivalence in MeV . ( $m_p=1.007277$ u, $m_n=1.008665$ u, $\left.1 \mathrm{u}=931.5 \mathrm{MeV} / \mathrm{c}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Mass defect: The mass defect is the difference between the total mass of all the protons and neutrons (when they are separate) and the actual mass of the nucleus (when they are together).

$$ \Delta_m=(Z m_p+N m_n)-M_{\text{nucleus}} $$

Here:

$$ \begin{aligned} Z & =\text{number of protons} \\ N & =\text{number of neutrons} \\ m_p & =\text{mass of a proton} \\ m_n & =\text{mass of a neutron} \\ M_{\text{nucleus}} & =\text{actual mass of the nucleus} \end{aligned} $$

The missing mass (the mass defect) turns into energy that holds the nucleus together. This is called binding energy and is explained by Einstein's equation $E=mc^2$.

Binding energy: This is the energy needed to break a nucleus into all its individual protons and neutrons. It comes from the mass defect and shows how tightly the nucleus is held together.

Fission process and binding energy per nucleon: Fission happens when a large nucleus splits into two smaller ones, releasing a lot of energy. After the split, each smaller nucleus has a higher binding energy per nucleon, which means they are more stable. The difference in binding energy per nucleon between the original and the smaller nuclei is given out as energy.

(b) Finding the mass defect and energy equivalence:

Step 1: Calculate the mass defect ($\Delta m$)

The mass defect is found by subtracting the deuteron's actual mass from the total mass of its individual proton and neutron:

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta m & = (m_p + m_n) - m_{\text{deuteron}} \\ & = (1.007277 + 1.008665) - 2.013553 \\ & = 2.015942 - 2.013553 \\ & = 0.002389\,\text{u} \end{aligned} $$

Step 2: Find the energy equivalent (in MeV)

Each atomic mass unit ($1\,\mathrm{u}$) equals $931.5\,\mathrm{MeV}/c^2$. Multiply the mass defect by this:

$$ \begin{aligned} E &= \Delta m \times 931.5\,\mathrm{MeV}/c^2 \\ &= 0.002389 \times 931.5 \\ &= 2.225\,\mathrm{MeV} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
List-I shows various functional dependencies of energy $(E)$ on the atomic number $(Z)$. Energies associated with certain phenomena are given in List-II.

Choose the option that describes the correct match between the entries in List-I to those in List-II.

List–I List–II
(P) $E \propto Z^2$ (1) energy of characteristic x-rays
(Q) $E \propto (Z - 1)^2$ (2) electrostatic part of the nuclear binding energy for stable nuclei with mass numbers in the range 30 to 170
(R) $E \propto Z(Z - 1)$ (3) energy of continuous x-rays
(S) $E$ is practically independent of $Z$ (4) average nuclear binding energy per nucleon for stable nuclei with mass number in the range 30 to 170
(5) energy of radiation due to electronic transitions from hydrogen-like atoms
questions.solution:
(P) For a hydrogen - like atom (atomic. number $z$ ), the energy of an

electron in the n-th orbit (Bohr model) is, $E_n=\frac{-13.6 z^2}{n^2} \mathrm{eV}$

So $E \propto z^2$

So energy of radiation due to electronic transitions from hydrogen like atoms can be written as

$$ \begin{aligned} & E=-13.6 z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & E \propto z^2 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { so }(p) \rightarrow(s) $$

(Q) Characteristic $x$-rays are emitted when an electron from a higher shell fills a vacancy in an inner shell (eg. K-shell). The energy of the emitted $x$-ray corresponds to the energy difference between the shells.

For $k$-shell $x$-rays,

$$ \text { L- shell }(n=2) \rightarrow k \text {-shell }(n=1) $$ transition occurs.

The energy levels, are modified by screening effects as the inner electron sees an effective nuclear charge of approximately $(z-1)$ due to screening by the other $k$-shell electron.

So using Bohr-like model with effective charge :

$$ E_1=\frac{-13.6(z-1)^2}{1^2}, E_2=\frac{-13.6(z-1)^2}{2^2} $$

So $\Delta E=E_2-E_1$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =13 \cdot 6(z-1)^2\left[\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & \Delta E \propto(z-1)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & E \propto(z-1)^2 \end{aligned} $$

So $($ a $) \longrightarrow(1)$

(R) $E \propto z(z-1)$

Let's find the electrostatic energy between, protons in the nucleus: A nucleus with atomic number z has z protons.

The electrostatic (coulomb) energy arises from the repulsion between each pair of protons.

So number of paps $=z_{c_2}=\frac{z(z-1)}{2}$

Coulomb energy between two protons separated by a distance $r$.

$$ E_1=\frac{k q_1 q_2}{r}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{e \cdot e}{r}=\frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} $$

So total electrostatic energy.

$$ \begin{aligned} E & =\text { number of pairs } \times E_1 \\ \Rightarrow E & =\frac{z(z-1)}{2} \cdot \frac{e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \\ \Rightarrow E & \propto z(z-1) \end{aligned} $$

So $(R) \longrightarrow(2)$

(s) The binding energy per nucleon is a measure of the stability of a nucleus and is given by the semi-empirical mass formula. For most nuclei (except very light ones), the average binding energy per nucleon is approximately constant, around $8-9 \mathrm{Mev}$ due to the saturation of nuclear forces. i.e., it is independent of $z$.

so ( S ) $\rightarrow$ (4)

Hence, option (C) is correct.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: If ${ }^n C_{n-r}+3 \cdot{ }^n C_{n-r+1}+3 \cdot{ }^n C_{n-r+2} +{ }^n C_{n-r+3}={ }^x C_r$, then the value of $x$ is
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { }{ }^n C_{n-r}+3 \cdot{ }^n C_{n-r+1}+3 \cdot{ }^n C_{n-r+2}+{ }^n C_{n-r+3}={ }^x C_r \\ & \Rightarrow\left({ }^n C_{n-r}+{ }^n C_{n-r+1}\right)+2 \cdot\left({ }^n C_{n-r+1}+{ }^n C_{n-r+2}\right) +\left({ }^n C_{n-r+2}+{ }^n C_{n-r+3}\right)={ }^n C_r \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+1}+2^{n+1} C_{n-r+2}+{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+3}={ }^x C_r \\ & {\left[\because{ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_{r-1}={ }^{n+1} C_r\right] } \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\left\{{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+1}+{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+2}\right\} +\left\{{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+2}+{ }^{n+1} C_{n-r+3}\right\}={ }^x C_r \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow{ }^{n+2} C_{n-r+2}+{ }^{n+2} C_{n-r+3}={ }^x C_r \\ & \Rightarrow{ }^{n+3} C_{n-r+3}={ }^x C_r \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\Rightarrow{ }^{n+3} C_r={ }^x C_r\,\,\,\,\,\,\left[\because{ }^{n+3} C_{n-r+3}={ }^{n+3} C_r\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \therefore \quad x=n+3 $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: What is the coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$.
questions.solution:
We have, $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\left\{(1+x)\left(1-x+x^2\right)\right\}^{40}(1+x) \\ & =\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}(1+x) \\ & =\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+x\left(1+x^3\right)^{40} \end{aligned} $$

∴ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $(1+x)^{41}\left(1-x+x^2\right)^{40}$

= Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $x\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}$

$=$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}+$ Coefficient of $x^{49}$ in $\left(1+x^3\right)^{40}$

= Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\sum_{r=0}^{40}{ }^{40} C_r x^{3 r}+$ Coefficient of $x^{50}$ in $\sum_{r=0}^{40}{ }^{40} C_r x^{3 r}=0+0=0$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If x=(1111)2​, y=(1001)2​ and z=(110)2​, then what is x3−y3−z3−3xyz equal to?
options: (100111)2
questions.solution:
1. Convert to decimal

    $x=(1111)_2=1\cdot2^3+1\cdot2^2+1\cdot2^1+1\cdot2^0=8+4+2+1=15$

    $y=(1001)_2=1\cdot2^3+0\cdot2^2+0\cdot2^1+1\cdot2^0=8+1=9$

    $z=(110)_2=1\cdot2^2+1\cdot2^1+0\cdot2^0=4+2=6$

  2. Observe the identity

    For any $a,b,c$,

    $$a^3+b^3+c^3-3abc=(a+b+c)\bigl(a^2+b^2+c^2-ab-bc-ca\bigr).$$

    Here take $a=x,\;b=-y,\;c=-z$.

    Then

    $$x^3+(-y)^3+(-z)^3-3\,x(-y)(-z) =\bigl(x-y-z\bigr)\times\bigl(\dots\bigr).$$

    But $x-y-z=15-9-6=0$, so the whole expression is $0$.

  3. Hence

    $$x^3-y^3-z^3-3xyz=0.$$

  4. In binary this is $(0)_2$.

Answer: Option D.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the number of rational terms in the expansion of $(\sqrt{3}+5^\frac{1}{4})^{12}$
questions.solution:
1. General term of the expansion of $(\sqrt{3}+5^{1/4})^{12}$ is

    $ T_{k+1} = \binom{12}{k}(\sqrt{3})^{\,12-k}\bigl(5^{1/4}\bigr)^{k} = \binom{12}{k}\,3^{\frac{12-k}{2}}\,5^{\frac{k}{4}}, \quad k=0,1,2,\dots,12. $

  2. For $T_{k+1}$ to be rational, both exponents must be integers:

    a) $\displaystyle \frac{12-k}{2}$ must be an integer

    b) $\displaystyle \frac{k}{4}$ must be an integer

  3. Condition (a) ⇒ $12-k$ is even ⇒ $k$ is even.

    Condition (b) ⇒ $k$ is a multiple of 4.

  4. Values of $k$ satisfying both:

    $k=0,4,8,12$.

  5. Number of rational terms = 4.

Answer: Option C.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If the sum of binomial coefficients in the expansion of (x+y)n is 256, then the greatest binomial coefficient occurs in which one of the following terms?
questions.solution:
1. Sum of binomial coefficients in $(x+y)^n$ is

    $$\sum_{k=0}^n \binom{n}{k} = 2^n.$$

    Given $2^n = 256$, so

    $$2^n = 2^8\implies n=8.$$

  2. The coefficients in the expansion of $(x+y)^8$ are

    $$\binom{8}{0},\binom{8}{1},\dots,\binom{8}{8}.$$

  3. For even $n$, the greatest binomial coefficient is

    $$\binom{n}{n/2} = \binom{8}{4}.$$

  4. The term $\binom{8}{4}x^{8-4}y^4=\binom{8}{4}x^4y^4$ is the $(4+1)$th term, i.e.\ the 5th term.

Answer: Fifth term. (Option C)

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If $k<(\sqrt{2}+1)^3<k+2,$ where k is a natural number, the value of k?
questions.solution:
1. Compute $(\sqrt{2}+1)^2$:

    $$(\sqrt{2}+1)^2 = 2 + 1 + 2\sqrt{2} = 3 + 2\sqrt{2}.$$

  2. Multiply by $\sqrt{2}+1$ to get the cube:

    $$(\sqrt{2}+1)^3 = (\sqrt{2}+1)\,(3 + 2\sqrt{2})$$

    $$= 3\sqrt{2} + 2\cdot2 + 3 + 2\sqrt{2}$$

    $$= 7 + 5\sqrt{2}.$$

  3. Approximate $5\sqrt{2}$:

    $$5\sqrt{2} \approx 5 \times 1.414 = 7.07$$

    Therefore

    $$(\sqrt{2}+1)^3 \approx 7 + 7.07 = 14.07.$$

  4. Find natural $k$ such that

    $$k < 14.07 < k+2.$$

    Checking integers:

  • If $k=13$, then $13 < 14.07 < 15$ ✔

  • Other options (11, 15, 17) fail to bracket 14.07 within a width of 2.

Answer: 13.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is the remainder when $5 ^ {99}$ is divided by 13?
questions.solution:
To find the remainder when $5^{99}$ is divided by $13$, we use modular arithmetic.

We need to find:

$$ 5^{99} \bmod 13 $$

Let us first find powers of $5$ modulo $13$:

$$ 5^1 = 5 \equiv 5 \pmod{13} $$

$$ 5^2 = 25 \equiv 25 - 13 = 12 \pmod{13} $$

$$ 5^4 = (5^2)^2 \equiv 12^2 = 144 \equiv 144 - 143 = 1 \pmod{13} $$

So we get:

$$ 5^4 \equiv 1 \pmod{13} $$

Now divide $99$ by $4$:

$$ 99 = 4 \times 24 + 3 $$

Therefore,

$$ 5^{99} = 5^{4 \times 24 + 3} = (5^4)^{24} \cdot 5^3 $$

Taking modulo $13$:

$$ 5^{99} \equiv (1)^{24} \cdot 5^3 \pmod{13} $$

$$ 5^{99} \equiv 5^3 = 125 \pmod{13} $$

Now divide $125$ by $13$:

$$ 125 = 13 \times 9 + 8 $$

So,

$$ 125 \equiv 8 \pmod{13} $$

Hence, the remainder is:

$$ \boxed{8} $$

So the correct option is Option 3.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: Which one of the following is the greatest coefficient in the expansion of $(1 + x) ^ {100}$ ?
questions.solution:
In the expansion of

$$ (1+x)^{100}, $$

the general term is

$$ T_{r+1}=\binom{100}{r}x^r. $$

So, the coefficient of $x^r$ is

$$ \binom{100}{r}. $$

We have to find which of the given coefficients is greatest.

The options correspond to:

  • coefficient of $x^{100}$ is $\binom{100}{100}$

  • coefficient of $x^{99}$ is $\binom{100}{99}$

  • coefficient of $x^{51}$ is $\binom{100}{51}$

  • coefficient of $x^{50}$ is $\binom{100}{50}$

Now, in binomial coefficients, the greatest coefficient in the expansion of $(1+x)^n$ occurs in the middle.

Since $100$ is even, the middle term is the

$$ \left(\frac{100}{2}+1\right) = 51\text{st term}, $$

which contains

$$ x^{50}. $$

Its coefficient is

$$ \binom{100}{50}, $$

and this is the greatest coefficient.

Also, using symmetry,

$$ \binom{100}{51}=\binom{100}{49}, $$

so it is smaller than $\binom{100}{50}$.

Therefore, the greatest coefficient is the coefficient of $x^{50}$.

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 4}} $$

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: The lactic acid that gets accumulated in the muscle cells during rigorous exercise causing muscle cramps is produced from
questions.solution:
The lactic acid formed in muscle under anaerobic (low-O₂) conditions comes from pyruvate. Specifically, lactate dehydrogenase catalyzes:

$$\text{pyruvate} + \text{NADH} + H^+ \;\longrightarrow\; \text{lactate} + \text{NAD}^+$$

So the correct choice is Option 2: Pyruvate.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: A chain of peptide containing linear sequences of amino acid linked by peptide bonds best represent the
questions.solution:
The description “a linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds” refers to the protein’s

Primary structure

Briefly:

Primary structure is simply the order of amino acids in the polypeptide chain.

Secondary structure involves local folding (α-helices, β-sheets).

Tertiary structure is the overall 3D shape of a single chain.

Quaternary structure is the assembly of multiple chains.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Cambium is an example of
questions.solution:
Cambium is a classic example of a lateral meristem—tissue that drives the thickening (secondary growth) of stems and roots.

Why it’s not the others:

Apical meristem (Option 2) occurs at shoot and root tips and is responsible for lengthwise growth.

Intercalary meristem (Option 3) is found at the base of nodes (common in grasses) and helps elongate internodes.

Permanent tissue (Option 4) consists of mature, non-dividing cells.

Answer: Option 1 – Lateral meristem.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which is the correct base pairing found in a normal DNA molecule?
questions.solution:
The correct pairing in normal DNA is:

Adenine (A) pairs with Thymine (T)

Cytosine (C) pairs with Guanine (G)

So the answer is Option 1: Adenine pairs with Thymine.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Clotting of blood involves which one among the following clotting proteins?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 2: Fibrinogen.

Explanation:

Fibrinogen (also called Factor I) is a soluble plasma protein.

During clotting, the enzyme thrombin cleaves fibrinogen to form insoluble fibrin strands.

These fibrin strands weave through the platelet plug, stabilizing it and forming the blood clot.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Initial discovery of antibiotic penicillin was done by
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Alexander Fleming.

Concept:

Antibiotics are compounds that either kill bacteria or inhibit their growth, significantly transforming medical treatment for bacterial infections.

The discovery of penicillin, the first widely used antibiotic, was a monumental breakthrough in medicine, saving countless lives.

Penicillin was discovered accidentally by Alexander Fleming in 1928. He observed that a mold named Penicillium notatum produced a substance that could inhibit bacterial growth.

Explanation of Correct Answer:

Alexander Fleming was a Scottish bacteriologist credited with discovering penicillin in 1928.

During his research, he noticed that colonies of the bacterium Staphylococcus aureus were destroyed by a mold contaminant on a culture plate.

The mold was identified as Penicillium notatum, and the substance it produced was named "penicillin." Fleming's discovery led to a Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1945, shared with Howard Florey and Ernst Boris Chain.

Other Options:

Francis Crick: Crick was a British molecular biologist and neuroscientist known for co-discovering the structure of DNA with James Watson in 1953.

Maurice Wilkins: A British physicist and molecular biologist, Wilkins contributed to the discovery of DNA's double-helix structure, working closely with Rosalind Franklin. He shared the 1962 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine with Watson and Crick.

Charles Darwin: A British naturalist, Darwin is renowned for his theory of evolution by natural selection, detailed in his 1859 work, On the Origin of Species.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: The vector of malaria parasites is
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 3: the female Anopheles mosquito.

• Only Anopheles mosquitoes can transmit Plasmodium (the malaria parasite).

• Only females take blood meals, picking up and passing on the parasite.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question:
Which of the following pairs is/are correctly matched?
1.Malaria : Mycobacterium
2.TB : Plasmodium
Select the answer using the code given below:
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 4: Neither 1 nor 2.

Malaria is caused by Plasmodium species (not Mycobacterium).

Tuberculosis (TB) is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis (not Plasmodium).

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Vitamin B1 is also known as
questions.solution:
Vitamin B₁ is also known as thiamin (sometimes spelled thiamine).

Riboflavin is vitamin B₂

Retinol is vitamin A

Tocopherol is vitamin E

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: The book ‘The Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection’ was authored by
questions.solution:
The correct answer is:

$$\text{Option 1: Charles Darwin}$$

Charles Darwin wrote The Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection. It was published in $1859$ and explained the theory of natural selection.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: The optimum pH at which the enzymes pepsin and trypsin of the human alimentary canal works are
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 1: pH $2.0$ and $7.9$, respectively.

Step-by-step:

  1. Pepsin works in the stomach.

  • The stomach has a highly acidic medium due to hydrochloric acid.

  • So, pepsin works best at about pH $2.0$.

  1. Trypsin works in the small intestine.

  • The small intestine has an alkaline medium.

  • So, trypsin works best at about pH $7.9$.

Therefore,

$$ \text{Pepsin} \rightarrow pH\,2.0,\qquad \text{Trypsin} \rightarrow pH\,7.9 $$

So, the answer is Option 1.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: The amount of air breathed in and out during a normal respiration by human lungs is referred to as
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 2: Tidal volume.

Here is a step-by-step explanation of the terms based on the NCERT textbook to help you understand why this is the correct answer:

1. Tidal Volume (TV)

According to your textbook, Tidal Volume is defined as the volume of air inspired or expired during a normal respiration. For a healthy man, this volume is approximately $500 \text{ mL}$ per inspiration or expiration. Therefore, this matches the description given in your question perfectly.

Let us also look at the other options so you can understand what they mean:

2. Vital Capacity (VC)

This is the maximum volume of air a person can breathe in after a forced expiration. It includes the Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV), Tidal Volume (TV), and Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV). It is not the volume during normal respiration.

3. Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV)

This refers to the additional volume of air a person can inspire by a forcible inspiration, over and above the normal Tidal Volume. It averages $2500 \text{ mL}$ to $3000 \text{ mL}$.

4. Total Lung Capacity (TLC)

This is the total volume of air accommodated in the lungs at the end of a forced inspiration. It includes Residual Volume (RV), Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV), Tidal Volume (TV), and Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV).

Since the question specifically asks for the amount of air breathed in and out during normal respiration, Tidal volume is the correct biological term.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: Which one among the following statements about arteries and veins in humans is NOT correct ?
options: Arteries carry blood away from heart while veins bring blood towards the heart
Pulmonary arteries always carry oxygenated blood while Pulmonary veins always carry deoxygenated blood
Arteries have thicker and more elastic walls compared to veins in general
Arteries do not have valves but veins do have valves
questions.solution:
Pulmonary arteries always carry oxygenated blood while pulmonary veins always carry deoxygenated blood is NOT correct.

Explanation:

Role of Arteries and Veins:

Arteries and veins move blood in our body.

Usually, arteries carry oxygen-rich (oxygenated) blood from the heart to the body. Veins bring back oxygen-poor (deoxygenated) blood from the body to the heart.

Exception in Pulmonary Circulation:

In the case of the lungs, this rule is opposite:

The pulmonary artery takes deoxygenated blood from the heart (right ventricle) to the lungs, so it can get oxygen.

The pulmonary vein brings oxygenated blood from the lungs back to the heart (left atrium).

So, here arteries carry deoxygenated blood and veins carry oxygenated blood, which is the opposite of the usual rule.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: Identify the functions that are controlled by the hind brain of human being
options: Involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation vomiting ete.
Controlling the regions receiving sensory impulses from receptors
Controlling the movement of voluntary muscles
Sensation of hunger or being full
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 1: Involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation vomiting etc.

Let us understand this step-by-step by looking at the divisions of the human brain as described in your NCERT textbook:

  1. The human brain is divided into three major parts or regions: the fore-brain, mid-brain, and hind-brain.

  2. The hind-brain plays a major role in controlling involuntary actions (actions that happen without our conscious thought). It consists of three parts: the pons, medulla, and cerebellum.

According to the NCERT textbook, involuntary actions such as blood pressure, salivation, and vomiting are strictly controlled by the medulla present in the hind-brain. Therefore, Option 1 is correct.

Let us also look at the other options to understand why they are incorrect:

  • Option 2 (Sensory impulses): The fore-brain is the main thinking part of the brain. It possesses specialized regions that receive sensory impulses from various receptors for hearing, smell, sight, etc.

  • Option 3 (Voluntary muscles): The fore-brain contains motor areas that control the movement of voluntary muscles (like your leg muscles when you decide to walk). While a part of the hind-brain (the cerebellum) helps in the precision and balance of these movements, the actual control and decision of voluntary movement come from the fore-brain.

  • Option 4 (Sensation of hunger): The sensation of feeling hungry or being full is controlled by a separate, specific centre associated with the fore-brain.

Thus, comparing all the functions, Option 1 is the correct function controlled by the hind-brain.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: Iodized salt is a vital part of our diet as it is essential for the synthesis of
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 2: Thyroxin

Explanation:

  1. Iodine is a micronutrient required by our body in very small amounts.

  2. The thyroid gland, located in the neck, uses iodine to produce the hormone thyroxin.

  3. Thyroxin controls the metabolic rate of the body and helps in growth and development.

  4. A deficiency of iodine leads to goitre, a disease in which the thyroid gland enlarges.

Therefore, iodized salt is included in our diet to provide iodine for the synthesis of thyroxin.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: The menstrual cycle in females is controlled by hormones that are secreted by
questions.solution:
The correct option is:

Option 3: Ovary and pituitary gland

Explanation:

  1. The pituitary gland (present in the brain) secretes two important hormones:

  • Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH)

  • Luteinising Hormone (LH)

    These hormones act on the ovaries.

  1. The ovaries respond to these hormones and secrete:

  • Oestrogen

  • Progesterone

  1. Together, these hormones regulate different stages of the menstrual cycle — the maturation of the egg, release of the egg (ovulation), and preparation of the uterus for possible pregnancy.

Hence, the menstrual cycle is controlled by the ovary and pituitary gland.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: Which one of the following blood cells type secrete large number of antibodies during am immune response of the body?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is:

Option 3: Lymphocytes

Explanation:

During an immune response, B-lymphocytes get activated and change into plasma cells. These plasma cells secrete a large number of antibodies.

Why not the others?

  • Neutrophils: They mainly engulf and destroy microbes.

  • Eosinophils: They are involved in allergic reactions and defense against parasites.

  • Monocytes: They become macrophages and help in phagocytosis.

So, the blood cells that secrete large numbers of antibodies are lymphocytes.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question: Which of the following organelles have their own DNA and ribosomes ?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option 2: Mitochondria and Chloroplast.

Explanation:

Mitochondria and chloroplast are called semi-autonomous organelles because they have:

  • their own DNA

  • their own ribosomes

This helps them to make some of their own proteins.

Why not the other options?

  • Endoplasmic reticulum and Golgi bodies do not have their own DNA or ribosomes.

  • Cytoskeleton does not have DNA or ribosomes.

  • Cell wall is a non-living covering, and nucleolus does not have its own DNA and ribosomes like mitochondria and chloroplast.

Thus, the answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{Mitochondria and Chloroplast}}$$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: Angular stomatitis and cracked lips are caused by deficiency of
questions.solution: Riboflavin ( $\mathrm{B}_2$ ) deficiency leads to cheilosis (cracks at mouth corners), glossitis, and digestive issues. Vitamin A is related to vision; C for scurvy; Niacin for pellagra.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A tetrapeptide is formed from four amino acids: alanine, serine, glycine, and valine. The C-terminal is fixed as alanine, and the N-terminal amino acid is chiral. Determine the number of possible sequences.
questions.solution:
Given that, alanine is the C-terminal amino acid and N-terminal must be chiral. So, glycine is not used at the N-terminal because it is achiral. So, possible sequence of tetrapeptides are

Val-Gly-Ser-Ala

Val-Ser-Gly-Ala

Ser-Gly-Val-Ala

Ser-Val-Gly-Ala

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
A linear octasaccharide (molar mass $=1024 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) on complete hydrolysis produces three monosaccharides: ribose, 2-deoxyribose and glucose. The amount of 2-deoxyribose formed is $58.26 \%(\mathrm{w} / \mathrm{w})$ of the total amount of the monosaccharides produced in the hydrolyzed products. The number of ribose unit(s) present in one molecule of octasaccharide is $\qquad$ .

Use: Molar mass $\left(\right.$ in g $\left.\mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ : ribose $=150,2$-deoxyribose $=134$, glucose $=180$;

Atomic mass (in amu): $\mathrm{H}=1, \mathrm{O}=16$
questions.solution:
The problem involves determining the composition of an octasaccharide that, upon complete hydrolysis, yields three types of monosaccharides: ribose, 2-deoxyribose, and glucose.

First, consider the balanced chemical equation for the hydrolysis of the octasaccharide:

$ \text{Octasaccharide} + 7 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ribose} + \text{2-deoxyribose} + \text{Glucose} $

The initial molar mass of the octasaccharide is $1024 \, \text{g/mol}$, and it requires 7 water molecules (each with a molar mass of $18 \, \text{g/mol}$, thus totaling $126 \, \text{g/mol}$) to undergo hydrolysis. Therefore, the total mass on the reactant side is:

$ 1024 + 126 = 1150 \, \text{g} $

According to the given data, the 2-deoxyribose formed constitutes $58.26\%$ of the total mass of the monosaccharides. To find the mass of 2-deoxyribose, calculate:

$ 1150 \times \frac{58.26}{100} = 669.99 \, \text{g} \approx 670 \, \text{g} $

The molar mass of 2-deoxyribose is $134 \, \text{g/mol}$, so the number of units produced is:

$ \frac{670}{134} = 5 \, \text{units} $

Assuming there is one unit of glucose (molar mass $180 \, \text{g/mol}$), the remaining units in the octasaccharide must be ribose. Given five units of 2-deoxyribose and one unit of glucose, the potential number of ribose units can be calculated as the difference to reach a total of eight saccharide units, ensuring:

$ 5 \text{ (2-deoxyribose)} + 1 \text{ (glucose)} + x \text{ (ribose)} = 8 $

Solving this gives:

$ x = 8 - 5 - 1 = 2 $

To verify the setup, the total mass of the hydrolysis products equals the mass at the reactant side:

$ 670 \, \text{(2-deoxyribose)} + 180 \, \text{(glucose)} + (2 \times 150 \, \text{(ribose)}) = 1150 \, \text{g} $

Thus, the octasaccharide contains 2 ribose units. Therefore, the number of ribose units present in one molecule of the octasaccharide is 2.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question:
A dielectric slab of dielectric constant $k=3$ is filling $\frac{3}{4}$ th space of the capacitor.

When capacitor is charged, then \% of total energy stored in dielectric is
questions.solution:
Energy density in air gap $=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2=u_E$

Energy density of dielectric $=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 K \frac{E^2}{K^2}=\frac{u_E}{K}$

Now, energy stored $=$ energy density × volume

∴ Total energy stored

$=\frac{3}{4} \cdot x \cdot \frac{u_E}{K}+\frac{1}{4} \cdot x \cdot u_E[x=$ volume of capacitor $]$

$=x u_E\left(\frac{3}{4 K}+\frac{1}{4}\right) \therefore K=3$;

$=x u_E\left(\frac{2}{4}\right)=\frac{x u_E}{2}$

Energy stored in dielectric $=\frac{3}{4} \cdot x \cdot \frac{u_E}{K}=\frac{x u_E}{4}$

So, \% of energy stored in the dielectric

$$ =\frac{\frac{x u_E}{4}}{\frac{x u_E}{2}} \times 100=50 \% $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) A parallel plate capacitor is charged by an ac source. Show that the sum of conduction current $\left(I_c\right)$ and the displacement current $\left(I_d\right)$ has the same value at all points of the circuit.

(b) In case (a) above, is Kirchhoff's first rule (junction rule) valid at each plate of the capacitor? Explain.
questions.solution:
(a) Electric field between the capacitor plates is given by

$$ E=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0 A} $$

where $q$ is the charge accumulated on the positive plate.

The electric flux through this plate is

$$ \phi_E=E A=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0 A} \cdot A=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0} $$

∴ Displacement current,

$$ I_D=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi}{d t}=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}\right]=\frac{d q}{d t} $$

But $\frac{d q}{d t}=$ the rate at which charge flows to positive plate through the conducting wire.

Hence   $$ I_D=I_C $$

i.e., Displacement current between capacitor plates $=$ Conduction current in connecting wires.

(b) Yes, Kirchhoff's first rule (junction rule) is valid at each plate of the capacitor. According to the junction rule, the sum of currents entering any junction must be equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction.

In the case of a capacitor, the conduction current and the displacement current must be balanced. This ensures that the total current entering the capacitor is equal to the total current leaving it, maintaining the continuity of current in the circuit.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
A circuit consisting of a capacitor $C$, a resistor of resistance $R$ and an ideal battery of emf $V$, as shown in figure is known as $R C$ series circuit.

As soon as the circuit is completed by closing key $S_1$ (keeping $S_2$ open) charges begin to flow between the

capacitor plates and the battery terminals. The charge on the capacitor increases and consequently the potential difference $V_c(=q / C)$ across the capacitor also increases with time. When this potential difference equals the potential difference across the battery, the capacitor is fully charged $(Q=V C)$. During this process of charging, the charge $q$ on the capacitor changes with time $t$ as $q=Q \left[1-e^{-t / R C}\right]$

The charging current can be obtained by differentiating it and using $\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^{m x}\right)=m e^{m x}$

Consider the case when $R=20 \mathrm{k} \Omega, C=500 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ and $V=10$ V.

(i) The final charge on the capacitor, when key $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open, is

(A) $5 \mu \mathrm{C}$

(B) 5 mC

(C) 25 mC

(D) 0.1 C

(ii) For sufficient time the key $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open. Now key $S_2$ is closed and $S_1$ is open. What is the final charge on the capacitor?

(A) Zero

(B) 5 mC

(C) 2.5 mC

(D) $5 \mu \mathrm{C}$

(iii) The dimensional formula for $R C$ is

(A) $\left[M L^2 T^{-3} A^{-2}\right]$

(B) $\left[M^0 L^0 T^{-1} A^0\right]$

(C) $\left[M^{-1} L^{-2} T^4 A^2\right]$

(D) $\left[M^0 L^0 T A^0\right]$

(iv) The key $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open. The value of current in the resistor after 5 seconds, is

(A) $\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{e}} \mathrm{~mA}$

(B) $\sqrt{e} \mathrm{~mA}$

(C) $\frac{1}{\sqrt{e}} \mathrm{~mA}$

(D) $\frac{1}{2 e} \mathrm{~mA}$
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} q & =Q\left(1-e^{-t / R C}\right) \\ R & =20 \mathrm{k} \Omega \\ C & =500 \mu \mathrm{~F} \\ V & =10 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

(i) Option (B) is correct.

Explanation: The final charge on the capacitor, when the key $S_1$ is closed and $S_2$ is open:

$Q=C V$ (As the battery will charge capacitor to its full capacity)

$$ \begin{aligned} & Q=500 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \\ & Q=5 \mathrm{mC} . \end{aligned} $$

(ii) Option (A) is correct.

Explanation: As $S_1$ is open, the capacitor will discharge through resistor completely. So, final charge on the capacitor is zero.

(iii) Option (D) is correct.

$$ \begin{aligned}Explanation: {[R C] } & =\left[M L^2 T^{-3} A^{-2}\right]\left[M^{-1} L^{-2} T^4 A^2\right] \\ & =\left[M^0 L^0 T A^0\right] \end{aligned} $$

(iv) Option (A) is correct.

$$ \begin{aligned} Explanation:& q=Q\left(1-e^{-t / R C}\right) \\ & I=\frac{d q}{d t} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { Time constant } & =R C=20 \mathrm{k} \Omega \times 500 \mu \mathrm{~F}=10 \\ I & =5 \times \frac{e^{-\frac{5}{10}}}{10}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{e}} \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$

OR

(iv) Option (B) is correct.

$$ \begin{aligned}Explanation: I & =\frac{d q}{d t}=Q\left(0-e^{-t / \tau}\left(-\frac{1}{\tau}\right)\right) \\ I & =\frac{Q}{\tau} e^{-\frac{t}{\tau}} \\ \text { ⇒ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,I & =\frac{C V}{R C} e^{-\frac{t}{\tau}} \\ I & =\frac{V}{R} e^{-\frac{t}{\tau}} \\ & =I_o e^{-\frac{t}{\tau}} \\ I_0 & =\frac{V}{R} \\ & =\frac{10 \mathrm{~V}}{20 \mathrm{k} \Omega}=0.5 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned} $$
