questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: Two wires of the same material (Young's modulus $=Y$ ) and same length $L$ but radii $R$ and $2 R$ respectively are joined end to end and a weight $w$ is suspended from the combination as shown in the figure. The elastic potential energy in the system is
questions.solution: $$ \text { } \begin{aligned} \Delta l_1 & =\frac{w L}{\left(4 \pi R^2\right) y}, \Delta l_2=\frac{w L}{\pi R^2 Y} \\ \therefore U & =\frac{1}{2} K_1\left(\Delta l_1\right)^2+\frac{1}{2} K_2\left(\Delta l_2\right)^2\left(K=\frac{Y A}{L}\right) \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{Y\left(4 \pi R^2\right)}{L} \times\left[\frac{w L}{4 \pi R^2 Y}\right]^2+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{Y\left(\pi R^2\right)}{L} \times\left[\frac{w L}{\pi R^2 Y}\right]^2\\ & =\frac{5 w^2 L}{8 \pi R^2 Y} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A current of 4.0 A is passed through 0.5 L of 0.2 M NaCl solution for 1200 s . Calculate the pH of the solution after electrolysis.
questions.solution:
Electrolysis of NaCl produces NaOH at the cathode.

Charge passed $=4 \times 1200=4800 \mathrm{C}$

Moles of electrons $=\frac{4800}{96500} \approx 0.04974 \mathrm{~mol}$

Since 2 moles of electron produce 2 moles NaOH , NaOH formed $\boldsymbol{\approx} 0.04974 \mathrm{~mol}$

$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Concentration }=\frac{0.04974}{0.5}=0.0995 \mathrm{M} \\ \text { pOH }=-\log 0.0995 \\ \text { pOH } \approx 1, \text { so } \mathrm{pH} \approx 14-1=13 \end{gathered} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The approximate time duration in hours to electroplate 20 g of calcium from molten calcium chloride using a current of 4 A is (Atomic mass of $\mathrm{Ca}=40$ )
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} &\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { } \mathrm{Ca}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \quad \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ca} \text { (at cathode) } \\ & 1 \text { mole of } \mathrm{CaCl}_2=2 \mathrm{~F} \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\, \quad Q=I t \Rightarrow n \cdot 2 F=I t \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t=\frac{n \cdot 2 F}{I}=\frac{20}{40} \times \frac{2 \times 96500}{4} \\ &  \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=24125 \mathrm{~s}=6.7 \text { hours. } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
In an electrochemical cell, dichromate ions in aqueous acidic medium are reduced to Cr3+. The current (in amperes) that flows through the cell for 48.25 minutes to produce 1 mole of Cr3+ is ______.

Use: 1 Faraday = 96500 C mol−1
questions.solution:
dichromate ion $\xrightarrow[\text { medium }]{\text { aqueous acidic }} \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ ion (reduction)
time required, $t=48.25 \mathrm{~min}$
moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}=1 \mathrm{~mol}$
1 Faraday $=96500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$
Faraday's laws of electrolysis is used hare.
The reduction of dichromate ion $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7{ }^{2-}$ to $\mathrm{Cr}^{8+}$ In acidic medium can be written as chemical equation:
$$ \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} 1+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$
From this equation, 6 moles of electrons are required to produce 2 moles of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$. So, for 1 mole of $\mathrm{Cr}^3, 3$ moles ot electrons are required.
$$ \left\{\begin{array}{l} 2 mol \, c r^3+ \rightarrow 6 e^{-} \\ 1 mol\, c r^{3+} \rightarrow \frac{6}{2} e^{-}=3 e^{-} \end{array}\right. $$
Total charge can be calculated as $$ Q=n \times F $$
$n \rightarrow$ moles of electrons
$F \rightarrow$ Faraday's constant
$$ \begin{aligned} Q & =3 \mathrm{~mol} \times 96500 \mathrm{cmol}^{-1} \\ & =289500 \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$
Time given in minutes convert to seconds as
$$ \begin{aligned} t & =48.25 \min \times \frac{60 \mathrm{~s}}{1 \mathrm{~min}} \\ & =2895 \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned} $$
Current is calculated using the formula (law of dectrolysis, Faraday's)
$$ \begin{array}{rlr} I & =\frac{Q}{t} \quad(Q=I x t) \\ & =\frac{289500 \mathrm{C}}{2895 \mathrm{~s}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,C s^{-1}=A \text { (Ampere) }\\ & =100 \mathrm{Cs}^{-1} \\ & =100 \mathrm{~A} \end{array} $$
Answer: 100 Ampere

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
An electrochemical cell is fueled by the combustion of butane at 1 bar and 298 K . Its cell potential is $\frac{\boldsymbol{X}}{F} \times 10^3$ volts, where $F$ is the Faraday constant. The value of $\boldsymbol{X}$ is _____________.

Use: Standard Gibbs energies of formation at 298 K are: $\Delta_f G_{\mathrm{CO}_2}^o=-394 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} ; \Delta_f G_{\text {water }}^o=$ $-237 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} ; \Delta_f G_{\text {butane }}^o=-18 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
questions.solution:
Balanced combustion of butane (liquid water):

$$\mathrm{C_4H_{10}} + \tfrac{13}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2}\;\longrightarrow\;4\,\mathrm{CO_2} + 5\,\mathrm{H_2O}$$

Standard Gibbs energy change:

$$\Delta G^\circ =\bigl[4\,\Delta_fG^\circ(\mathrm{CO_2})+5\,\Delta_fG^\circ(\mathrm{H_2O})\bigr]\,-\bigl[\Delta_fG^\circ(\mathrm{C_4H_{10}})+\tfrac{13}{2}\,\Delta_fG^\circ(\mathrm{O_2})\bigr]$$

$$= \bigl[4(-394)+5(-237)\bigr]\,-[-18+0]\;\mathrm{kJ/mol} = -1576 -1185 +18 = -2743\;\mathrm{kJ/mol}$$

Electrons transferred, $n$:

Each C goes from –2.5 to +4 ⇒ loses 6.5 e–, so total $n=4\times6.5=26$.

Cell potential:

$\begin{aligned} & \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}=-\mathrm{nFE}^{\circ} \\ & -2743 \times 1000=-26 \times \mathrm{FE}^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=\frac{105.5}{\mathrm{~F}} \times 10^3=105.50\end{aligned}$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A current $I=10 \sin (100 \pi t) A$ is passed in the first coil, which induces a maximum emf of $5 \pi \mathrm{~V}$ in second coil. The mutual inductance between the coil is
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} e & =-M \frac{d i}{d t} \\ \frac{d i}{d t} & =10 \times 100 \pi \cos (100 \pi t) \end{aligned} $$

Maximum value of $\frac{d i}{d t}$ is $10 \times 100 \pi \cdot[\because \cos (100 \pi t)=1]$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned} Now,\, M & =\frac{e}{\frac{d i}{d t}}=\frac{5 \pi}{10 \times 100 \pi} \\ & =5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{H}=5 \mathrm{mH} \\ M & =5 \mathrm{mH} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: A coil has 100 turns, each of area $0.05 \mathrm{~m}^2$ and total resistance $1.5 \Omega$. It is inserted at an instant in a magnetic field of 90 mT , with its axis parallel to the field. The charge induced in the coil at that instant is
questions.solution:
Step 1: List What We Know

  • Number of turns ($N$) = 100
  • Area of each turn ($A$) = $0.05~\mathrm{m}^2$
  • Total resistance ($R$) = $1.5~\Omega$
  • Magnetic field ($B$) = $90~\mathrm{mT}$ = $90 \times 10^{-3}~\mathrm{T}$

Step 2: Find Initial and Final Magnetic Flux

The initial magnetic flux through the coil is: $$\phi_{B i} = B \times A = (90 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.05) = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}~\mathrm{Wb} $$

The final magnetic flux after inserting the coil is: $$\phi_{B f} = 0~\mathrm{Wb} $$

Step 3: Calculate the Change in Flux

$$\Delta \phi_B = \phi_{B f} - \phi_{B i} = 0 - 4.5 \times 10^{-3} = -4.5 \times 10^{-3}~\mathrm{Wb}$$

Step 4: Use Faraday’s Law and Find Total Induced Charge

Faraday’s Law says: $$ e = -N \frac{\Delta \phi_B}{\Delta t} $$

The total charge $Q$ that flows is: $$ Q = I \Delta t = \frac{\varepsilon \Delta t}{R} = -N \frac{\Delta \phi_B}{R} $$

Step 5: Substitute the Values

Plug in the numbers: $$ Q = \frac{100 \times (-4.5 \times 10^{-3})}{1.5} $$

Calculate the result: $$ Q = \frac{-0.45}{1.5} = -0.3~\mathrm{C} $$

Since charge is asked as an amount, we just write $0.3~\mathrm{C}$.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
Assertion (A): It is difficult to move a magnet into a coil of large number of turns when the circuit of the coil is closed.

Reason (R): The direction of induced current in a coil with its circuit closed, due to motion of a magnet, is such that it opposes the cause.
options: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason $(R)$ is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
When you try to move a magnet into a coil that has many loops (turns) and the circuit is closed, it feels hard to push the magnet in.

This is because, as the magnet moves into the coil, an electric current is created (induced) in the coil.

This electric current makes its own magnetic field, which pushes back against the magnet you are moving in. This makes it harder to move the magnet.

This effect is explained by Lenz's law. Lenz's law says that the induced current will always flow in a direction that tries to stop the thing (the moving magnet) that created it.

So, the coil acts to resist the magnet being moved in by creating a force that pushes in the opposite direction.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) Show that the energy required to build up the current $I$ in a coil of inductance $L$ is $\frac{1}{2} L I^2$.

(b) Considering the case of magnetic field produced by air-filled current carrying solenoid, show that the magnetic energy density of a magnetic field $B$ is $\frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0}$.
questions.solution:
(a) Energy required to build up current in an inductor

When you make a current flow through a coil (or inductor), the coil creates an opposing voltage (called back or induced emf) that resists the change in current. The energy you use to overcome this resistance is stored in the coil as magnetic potential energy.

The power (work done per second) to increase the current $i$ in the coil is given by

$$ \frac{d W}{d t} = |\varepsilon| I = \left(L \frac{dI}{dt}\right) I $$

Here, $L$ is the inductance and $I$ is the current. $|\varepsilon|$ is the induced emf.

To find the total work needed to bring the current from $0$ up to $I$, integrate both sides:

$$ W = \int dW = \int_{0}^{I} L I dI = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 $$

This shows that the energy stored in an inductor when the current becomes $I$ is $ \frac{1}{2} L I^2 $.

(b) Magnetic energy density in an air-filled solenoid

In an empty (air-filled) solenoid, the energy stored in the magnetic field depends on the strength of the field $B$.

The permittivity of free space ($\varepsilon_0$), the permeability of free space ($\mu_0$), the electric field ($E$), and the speed of light ($c$) are related by:

$$ \varepsilon_0 = \frac{1}{c^2 \mu_0} $$

Also, in an electromagnetic wave, the electric field $E$ and magnetic field $B$ are connected by $E = Bc$.

The energy stored in an electric field inside a capacitor per unit volume is: $$ \frac{U}{A l} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^2 $$ where $U$ is the energy stored, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $l$ is the length.

If you put $E = Bc$ and $\varepsilon_0$ into the equation, you get: $$ U = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{c^2 \mu_0} \times (B c)^2 \times A l $$

This simplifies to: $$ u = \frac{U}{A l} = \frac{B^2}{2 \mu_0} $$ where $u$ is the energy density (energy per unit volume) stored in the magnetic field.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: A conducting square loop initially lies in the $X Z$ plane with its lower edge hinged along the $X$-axis. Only in the region $y \geq 0$, there is a time dependent magnetic field pointing along the $Z$-direction, $\vec{B}(t)=B_0(\cos \omega t) \hat{k}$, where $B_0$ is a constant. The magnetic field is zero everywhere else. At time $t=0$, the loop starts rotating with constant angular speed $\omega$ about the $X$ axis in the clockwise direction as viewed from the $+X$ axis (as shown in the figure). Ignoring self-inductance of the loop and gravity, which of the following plots correctly represents the induced e.m.f. $(V)$ in the loop as a function of time:
questions.solution:
Here, we will use the magnetic flux formula and Faraday's law. Faraday's law relates the induced EMF to the rate of change of magnetic flux, which is appropriate for a loop in a changing magnetic field and changing orientation.

magnetic flux formula: $\phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$

$$ \Rightarrow \phi=B A \cos \theta $$

and the induced EMF is given by Faraday's law: $\quad v=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}$

where, $\phi=$ magnetic flux through the loop

$B=$ magnetic field

$A=$ Area of the loop

$\theta=$ Angle between a perpendicular vector to the area and the magnetic field.

Given: $\quad \vec{B}(t)=B_0(\cos \omega t) \hat{k}$

$$ \theta=\omega t $$

we know, $\quad \phi=\vec{B} \cdot \vec{A}$

$\begin{aligned} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,& =B A \cos (90-\theta) \\ \phi & =B A \sin \theta\end{aligned}$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \phi & =B_0 \cos (\omega t) A \sin (\omega t) \\ \Rightarrow \phi & =\frac{B_0 A}{2} \sin (2 \omega t) \\ \varepsilon=v & =\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t}\left[\frac{B_0 A}{2} \sin (2 \omega t)\right] \\ & =\frac{-B_0 A}{2}(2 \omega) \cos (2 \omega t) \\ \Rightarrow \varepsilon & =V=-B_0 \omega A \cos (2 \omega t) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { (only for half rotation) } \end{aligned} $$

cos function with time period $=\frac{2 \pi}{2 w}$

$$ =\frac{\pi}{w} $$

Hence, option (A) is correct.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: A conducting square loop of side $L$, mass $M$ and resistance $R$ is moving in the $X Y$ plane with its edges parallel to the $X$ and $Y$ axes. The region $y \geq 0$ has a uniform magnetic field, $\vec{B}=B_0 \widehat{k}$. The magnetic field is zero everywhere else. At time $t=0$, the loop starts to enter the magnetic field with an initial velocity $v_0 \hat{\jmath} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, as shown in the figure. Considering the quantity $K=\frac{B_0^2 L^2}{R M}$ in appropriate units, ignoring self-inductance of the loop and gravity, which of the following statements is/are correct:
options: If $v_0 = 1.5KL$, the loop will stop before it enters completely inside the region of magnetic field.
When the complete loop is inside the region of magnetic field, the net force acting on the loop is zero.
If $v_0 = \frac{KL}{10}$, the loop comes to rest at $t = \left(\frac{1}{K}\right) \ln\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)$.
If $v_0 = 3KL$, the complete loop enters inside the region of magnetic field at time $t = \left(\frac{1}{K}\right) \ln\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)$.
questions.solution:
Here, as the loop enters the field, the magnetic flux through the loop is changing. Changing magnetic flux, according to Faraday's law, induces an EMF in the loop. As the loop has some resistance
So the loop gets an induced current and

The magnetic force arises from the induced current interacting with the field.

Flux : $\phi=B_0$. (Area in Field)

$$ \Rightarrow \phi=B_0 \cdot L y $$

EMF: $\quad V=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}\left(B_0 L y\right)$.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad v=-B_0 L \frac{d y}{d t} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v=-B_0 L v \end{aligned} $$

current $(v>0$, so current direction based on Lenz's (law): $I=\frac{V}{R}=\frac{-B_0 v L}{R}$

Force: current in the bottom edge (in field) interacts with $\vec{B}$

so force on a segment: $F=B_0 I L$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow F=B_0\left(\frac{-B_0 v L}{R}\right) L \\ & \Rightarrow F=\frac{-B_0^2 L^2 v}{R} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Given, } K=\frac{B_0^2 L^2}{R M} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \text { or } \varepsilon & =-B_0 v L \\ I & =\frac{-B_0 v L}{R} \\ F & =B_0 I L=B_0\left(\frac{-B_0 v L}{R}\right) L \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow \frac{m d v}{d t}=\frac{-\left (B_0^2 L^2\right) v}{R} \quad\left[\begin{array}{ll} \text { as } & F=m a \\ & =m \frac{d v}{d t} \end{array}\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \text { } \frac{mvd v}{d x}=\frac{-\left(B_0^2 L^2\right)}{R} {v} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d v}{d x}=-\frac{B_0^2 L^2}{M R} \\ & \Rightarrow \int_{v_0}^v d v=-K \int_0^{x} dx\\ & \Rightarrow v-v_0=-K x \\ & \text { If } v_0=1.5 \mathrm{KL} \text { then for } x=L \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow v-1.5 \mathrm{KL}=-K L \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v=1.5 \mathrm{KL}-\mathrm{KL}=0.5 \mathrm{KL} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad v=0.5 \mathrm{KL} \end{aligned} $$

So, the loop will have some velocity, and it will not stop.

If the loop completely gets inside then $\Phi$ becomes constant so $\frac{d \Phi}{d t}=\varepsilon=0, \quad I=0$
i.e., no current flows and no force acts.

if $v_0=\frac{K L}{10}$ then for $v=0$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 0+\frac{K L}{10}=-k x \Rightarrow x=\frac{L}{10} \\ & \frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{-B_0^2 L^2}{M R} v \\ & \Rightarrow \int_{v_0}^v \frac{d v}{v}=-k \int_0^t d t \\ & \Rightarrow[\ln |v|]_{v_0}^v=-k t \\ & \Rightarrow \ln \left|\frac{v}{v_0}\right|=-k t \\ & \Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{K} \ln \left|\frac{v_0}{v}\right| \\ & \text { for } v=0, t=\frac{1}{k} \ln (\infty)=\infty \end{aligned} $$

So it will never stop.

If $v_0=3 \mathrm{KL}$ then

$v$ at $x=L$,

$v-3 K L=-K L$

$$ \Rightarrow v=3 K L-K L=2 K L $$

So time, $t=\frac{1}{k} \ln \left|\frac{v_0}{v}\right|$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \quad t=\frac{1}{k} \ln \left|\frac{3 K L}{2 K L}\right| \\ & \Rightarrow t=\frac{1}{k} \ln \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Hence, Options (B) and (D) are correct.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A parallel plate capacitor with plate area $A$ and separation between plates $d$ is charged with constant current $I$. Consider a plane surface of area $\frac{5 A}{6}$ parallel to the plates and drawn between the plates. The displacement current through area is
questions.solution:
The charge on the capacitor plates at time $t$ is given by $q=I t$

Electric field $=\varepsilon=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0}=\frac{q}{A \varepsilon_0}=\frac{I t}{A \varepsilon_0}$

Flux through area $\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6}$ is $\phi_{\varepsilon}=\left(\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6}\right) \varepsilon$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad \phi_{\varepsilon}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~A}}{6} \times \frac{I t}{A \varepsilon_0} \Rightarrow \phi_{\varepsilon}=\frac{5 I t}{6 \varepsilon_0} $$

Now, displacement current $=I_d=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d \phi_e}{d t}$

                                         $$ \begin{aligned} & I_d=\varepsilon_0 \frac{d}{d t}\left[\frac{5 I t}{6 \varepsilon_0}\right] \\ & I_d=\frac{5}{6} I \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: The given diagram exhibits the relationship between the wavelength of the electromagnetic waves and the energy of photon associated with them. The three points $\mathbf{P}, \mathbf{Q}$ and $\mathbf{R}$ marked on the diagram may correspond respectively to
options:
X-rays, microwaves, UV radiation

X-rays, UV radiation, microwaves

UV radiation, microwaves, X-rays

Microwaves, UV radiation, X-rays
questions.solution:
Formula: $E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$

Energy and wavelength are inversely proportional. wavelength of microwave $>$ wavelength of UV $>$ wavelength of X-rays

$$ \begin{aligned}Hence,\,\,\, & P=\text { X-rays } \\ & R=\text { UV } \\ & Q=\text { Microwave } \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: A cube of unit volume contains $35 \times 10^7$ photons of frequency $10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}$. If the energy of all the photons is viewed as the average energy being contained in the electromagnetic waves within the same volume, then the amplitude of the magnetic field is $\alpha \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~T}$. Taking permeability of free space $\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{Tm} / \mathrm{A}$, Planck's constant $h=6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}$ and $\pi=\frac{22}{7}$, the value of $\alpha$ is ____________.
questions.solution:
As the cube has unit volume so energy density, $u=\frac{E}{1}=E$

where, $E=$ total energy

Energy of a photon, $E_1=h \nu$
So for n photon, $E=n h \nu$

Here, the question mentions "average energy contained in the electromagnetic waves within the same volume" which means we have to consider standing wave in a cavity (since the photons are in a cube).

Average energy density for a standing wave : $\langle u\rangle=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E_0{ }^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_0{ }^2}{\mu_0}\right)$

The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ comes from averaging $\sin ^2(k x)$

over the volume. $\left\langle\sin ^2(k x)\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2}$

For an $E M$ wave $E=C B$

$$ \text { so } E_0=C B_0 $$

Hence, $\langle u\rangle=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0\left(c B_0\right)^2+\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}\right)$

$$ =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 c^2 B_0^2+\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}\right) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\langle u\rangle=\frac{1}{2} & {\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{\mu_0 c^2}\right) c^2 B_0^2+\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}\right]\left(\begin{array}{l} As, c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \\ \text { so } \varepsilon_0=\frac{1}{\mu_0 c^2} \end{array}\right.} \\ \Rightarrow\langle u\rangle & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}+\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow\langle u\rangle=\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0} \end{aligned} $$

Given that this is equal to the photon energy density. so $\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}=n h \nu$

Total energy of photons.

$$ \begin{aligned} E & =n h \nu \\ & =35 \times 10^7 \times 6 \times 10^{-34} \times 1.15 \\ \Rightarrow E & =210 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned} $$

For $E M$ waves : $E=\frac{B_0{ }^2}{2 \mu_0}$

So $\frac{B_0^2}{2 \mu_0}=210 \times 10^{-12}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow B_0^2=2 \times 4 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 10^{-7} \times 210 \times 10^{-12} \\ & \Rightarrow B_0^2=5280 \times 10^{-19} \\ & \Rightarrow B_0^2=528 \times 10^{-18} \\ & \Rightarrow B_0=\sqrt{528} \times 10^{-9} \\ & \Rightarrow B_0=22.978 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~T} \\ & \\ & \text { = } \alpha \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~T} \text { (given) } \\ &So, \alpha=22.98 \text { Ans. } \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: When a $p-n$ junction diode is forward biased
options:
the barrier height and the depletion layer width both increase.

the barrier height increases and the depletion layer width decreases.

the barrier height and the depletion layer width both decrease.

the barrier height decreases and the depletion layer width increases.
questions.solution:
When a $p-n$ junction diode is forward biased, the external voltage helps charge flow across the junction.

This lowers the barrier height because the voltage works against the built-in electric field.

As a result, the depletion region becomes thinner, and it is easier for current to move through the diode.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
Assertion (A): We cannot form a $p-n$ junction diode by taking a slab of a $p$-type semiconductor and physically joining it to another slab of a $n$-type semiconductor.

Reason (R): In a $p$-type semiconductor $\eta_e \gg \eta_h$ while in a $n$-type semiconductor $\eta_h \gg \eta_{e^*}$
options: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason $(R)$ is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
Assertion (A): We cannot make a $p$-$n$ junction diode by simply placing a $p$-type semiconductor next to an $n$-type semiconductor. This statement is true.

To create a proper $p$-$n$ junction, the $p$-type and $n$-type materials need to touch each other at the tiniest, atomic level. This close connection lets electrons and holes move smoothly across the junction. If you just attach two separate pieces together, there will be a physical gap or barrier. This barrier stops the proper flow of electric current across the junction, so the device will not work as a normal $p$-$n$ junction.

Reason (R): The reason says: In a $p$-type semiconductor, $\eta_e \gg \eta_h$, and in an $n$-type semiconductor, $\eta_h \gg \eta_{e^*}$.

This reason is wrong. In a $p$-type semiconductor, there are many more holes ($\eta_h$) than electrons ($\eta_e$), so $\eta_h \gg \eta_e$. In an $n$-type semiconductor, electrons ($\eta_e$) are much more than holes ($\eta_h$), so $\eta_e \gg \eta_h$.

Therefore, Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: A $p$-type Si semiconductor is made by doping an average of one dopant atom per $5 \times 10^7$ silicon atoms. If the number density of silicon atoms in the specimen is $5 \times 10^{28}$ atoms $\mathrm{m}^{-3}$, find the number of holes created per cubic centimetre in the specimen due to doping. Also give one example of such dopants.
questions.solution:
Number density of atoms in Si

$$ \begin{aligned} & =5 \times 10^{28} \text { atoms m }{ }^{-3} \\ & =5 \times 10^{22} \text { atoms cm } \end{aligned} $$

Since, 1 atom of dopant is doped in $5 \times 10^7$ silicon atoms,

∴ Total number of dopant atoms

$$ =\frac{5 \times 10^{22}}{5 \times 10^7}=1 \times 10^{15} \text { atoms } \mathrm{cm}^{-3} $$

Since, each dopant atom creates a hole in a p-type semiconductor, the number of holes created per cubic centimetre is $=1 \times 10^{15}$

One example of such a dopant is boron (B), which is commonly used to create $p$-type semiconductors.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) Draw circuit arrangement for studying $V-I$ characteristics of a $p-n$ junction diode.

(b) Show the shape of the characteristics of a diode.

(c) Mention two information that you can get from these characteristics.
questions.solution:
(c) Information from $V$ to $I$ Characteristics:

Threshold Voltage: The minimum forward voltage required for the diode to conduct significantly ( $\approx 0.7 \mathrm{~V}$ for silicon, 0.3 V for germanium).

Breakdown Voltage: The reverse voltage at which the diode conducts heavily due to breakdown effects.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: Figure shows variation of Coulomb force $(F)$ acting between two point charges with $\frac{1}{r^2}, r$ being the separation between the two charges $\left(q_1, q_2\right)$ and $\left(q_2\right. q_3$ ). If $q_2$ is positive and least in magnitude, then the magnitudes of $q_1, q_2$ and $q_3$ are such that
questions.solution:
Given, $q_2$ is positive and least in magnitude.

From Coulomb's Force, $F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$

Now, from the given graph $F$ vs $1 / r^2$, the product of the magnitude of the charges will be the slope the curve.

$$ \begin{aligned} \because & & \tan \theta_1 & >\tan \theta_2 \\ \Rightarrow & & \theta_1 & >\theta_2 \end{aligned} $$

                       [if the given angle is very small]

                       $$q_1 q_2>q_2 q_3 $$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad q_1>q_3 $$

and $q_2$ has least magnitude.

Hence, $q_1>q_3>q_2$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) (i) Two point charges $5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at points ( $-3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0$ ) and ( $3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0$ ) respectively. An external electric field $\vec{E}=\frac{A}{r^2} \hat{r}$

where $A=3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Vm}$ is switched on in the region.

Calculate the change in electrostatic energy of the system due to the electric field.

(ii) A system of two conductors is placed in air and they have net charge of $+80 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-80 \mu \mathrm{C}$ which causes a potential difference of 16 V between them.

(1) Find the capacitance of the system.

(2) If the air between the capacitor is replaced by a dielectric medium of dielectric constant 3, what will be the potential difference between the two conductors?

(3) If the charges on two conductors are changed to $+160 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $-160 \mu \mathrm{C}$, will the capacitance of the system change?

Give reason for your answer.

OR

(b) (i) Consider three metal spherical shells $A, B$ and $C$, each of radius $R$. Each shell is having a concentric metal ball of radius $R / 10$. The spherical shells $\mathrm{A}, B$ and $C$ are given charges $+6 q,-4 q$ and $14 q$ respectively. Their inner metal balls are also given charges $-2 q$, $+8 q$ and $-10 q$ respectively. Compare the magnitude of the electric fields due to shells $A, B$ and $C$ at a distance $3 R$ from their centres.

(ii) A charge $-6 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is placed at the centre $B$ of a semicircle of radius 5 cm , as shown in the figure. An equal and opposite charge is placed at point D at a distance of 10 cm from B. A charge $+5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is moved from point ' $C$ ' to point ' $A$ ' along the circumference. Calculate the work done on the charge.
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { (i) Two point charges are given as: }\\ &\begin{aligned}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, q_1 & =5 \mu \mathrm{C}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { at }(-3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0) \\ & =(-0.03 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0) \\ q_2 & =-1 \mu \mathrm{C}=-1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{C} \text { at }(3 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0) \\ & =(0.03 \mathrm{~m}, 0,0) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

An external electric field is given by:

$$ E=\frac{A}{r^2}, A=3 \times 10^5 \mathrm{Vm} $$

The change in electrostatic energy of a system of charges in an external field is given by:

$$ \Delta U=-\sum q_i E\left(x_i\right) x_i $$

Since, the given electric field is position-dependent:

$$ E(x)=\frac{A}{x^2} $$

For charge $q_1=5 \mu \mathrm{C}$ at $x_1=-0.03 \mathrm{~m}$

$$ \begin{aligned} E_1 & =\frac{A}{x_1^2}=\frac{3 \times 10^5}{(-0.03)^2} \\ & =3.33 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \\For \,\,charge\,\,\, q_2 & =-1 \mu \mathrm{C} \text { at } x_2=0.03 \mathrm{~m} \\ E_2 & =\frac{A}{x_2^2}=\frac{3 \times 10^5}{(0.03)^2}=3.33 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Change in electrostatic energy: } \Delta U=\Delta U_1+\Delta U_2 $$

$$ \begin{gathered} \Delta U=-q_1 E_1 x_1-q_2 E_2 x_2 \\ \Delta U=-\left(5 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(3.33 \times 10^8\right)(-0.03)-\left(-1 \times 10^{-6}\right) \\ \left(3.33 \times 10^8\right)(0.0 .3)=0.06 \mathrm{~J} \end{gathered} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}(ii) (1)\,\, & C=Q / V \\ & C=\frac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{16}=5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}=5 \mu \mathrm{~F} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}(2)\,\, & C^{\prime}=K C=3 \times 5 \mu \mathrm{~F}=15 \mu \mathrm{~F} \\ & V^{\prime}=\frac{80 \times 10^{-6}}{15 \times 10^{-6}}=5.33 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

(3) Capacitance depends only on the physical properties (geometry, medium, plate separation), not on charge. Since, there is no change in plate configuration or medium, the capacitance remains $5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$.

Thus, the capacitance does not change.

OR

(i) Three conducting spherical shells $A, B$, and $C$, each of radius $R$, have inner concentric metal balls of radius $R / 10$.

Given charges: Shell A: $+6 q$, Inner Ball A: $-2 q$; Shell B: $-4 q$, Inner Ball B: $+8 q$; Shell C: $+14 q$, Inner Ball C: $-10 q$

Distance where the field is to be calculated: $3 R$.

For a conducting spherical shell, the total charge appears as if it is concentrated at the centre when calculating the field outside the shell. The inner ball induces charges on the inner surface of the shell, but the total charge on the outer shell remains the same. Thus, the net charge on each shell determines the electric field at $3 R$.

Shell A:

Net charge on the shell itself $=+6 q$.

Shell B:

Net charge on the shell itself $=-4 q$.

Shell C:

Net charge on the shell itself $=+14 q$.

Now the electric fields at a distance $3 R$ are given by

$$ \frac{k 6 q}{(3 R)^2}: \frac{k 4 q}{(3 R)^2}: \frac{k 14 q}{(3 R)^2} $$

6:4:14

i.e., $3: 2: 7$

(ii) Electric potential at any point due to a charge $q_{\text {at }}$ distance $r$ :

$$ V=k q / r $$

Since, $C$ and $A$ are at the same distance from $B$ (radius $=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ ), the potential at both points due to $B$ and $D$ is the same.

Thus, the potential difference between C and $A$ is zero, meaning:

$$ W=q \Delta V=0 $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
List-I shows four configurations, each consisting of a pair of ideal electric dipoles. Each dipole has a dipole moment of magnitude $p$, oriented as marked by arrows in the figures. In all the configurations the dipoles are fixed such that they are at a distance $2 r$ apart along the $x$ direction. The midpoint of the line joining the two dipoles is $X$. The possible resultant electric fields $\vec{E}$ at $X$ are given in List-II.

Choose the option that describes the correct match between the entries in List-I to those in List-II.

List–I List–II
(P) (1) $$ \vec{E}=0 $$
(Q) (2) $\displaystyle \vec{E} = -\,\frac{p}{2\pi\epsilon_0\,r^3}\,\hat{\jmath}$
(R) (3) $\displaystyle \vec{E} = -\,\frac{p}{4\pi\epsilon_0\,r^3}\,(\hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath})$
(S) (4) $\displaystyle \vec{E} = \frac{p}{4\pi\epsilon_0\,r^3}\,(2\hat{\imath} - \hat{\jmath})$
(5) $\displaystyle \vec{E} = \frac{p}{\pi\epsilon_0\,r^3}\,\hat{\imath}$
questions.solution:
The electric field $\vec{E}$ due to a dipole at a point is given by:

On the axial line (along the dipole axis): $E_{\text {axial }}=\frac{2 k p}{r^3}$ (along the dipole moment )

where, $K=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}$

On the equatorial line (perpendicular bisector):

$$ \text { Equatorial }=\frac{-k p}{r^3}\binom{\text { opposite to }}{\text { the dipole moment }} $$

Here, $x$ is on the equatorial line for both dipoles:

$$ \vec{E}=\overrightarrow{E_1}+\overrightarrow{E_2} $$

$$ \text { } \begin{aligned} & So\,\,\, \vec{E}=-\frac{k p}{r^3} \hat{j}-\frac{k p}{r^3} \hat{j} \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{E}=-\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{j}=\frac{-2}{r^3}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right) p{\hat{j}} \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{E}=\frac{-p}{2 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3} \hat{j} \\ & \text { So }(p) \rightarrow(2) \end{aligned} $$

Here, $x$ is on the equatorial line for both dipoles.

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Electric field at } x \text { due to dipole } 1 \text {, }\\ &\vec{E}_1=-\frac{k p}{r^3} \hat{j} \end{aligned} $$

Electric field at $x$ due to dipole 2,

$$ \vec{E}_2=\frac{k p}{r^3} \hat{j} \quad\left(\begin{array}{l} \text { as dipole moment } \\ \text { is in }-\hat{j} \text { direction }) \end{array}\right. $$

so. $\vec{E}=\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{-k p}{r^3} \hat{j}+\frac{k p}{r^3} \hat{j} \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{E}=0 \end{aligned} $$

So $($ Q $) \longrightarrow(1)$

Here, $x$ is on the equatorial

line for dipole 1 and on the axial line for dipole 2.

So, $\vec{E}_1=\frac{-k p}{r^3} \hat{j}$

and $\vec{E}_2=\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i}$

$$ \begin{aligned}So\,\,\,\,\, \vec{E} & =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2 \\ & =\frac{-k p}{r^3} \hat{j}+\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i} \\ & =\frac{k p}{r^3}(-\hat{j}+2 \hat{i}) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \vec{E}=\frac{k p}{r^3}(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}) \\ & \Rightarrow \vec{E}=\frac{p}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3}(2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { So }(R) \rightarrow(4) \end{aligned} $$

Here, $x$ is on the axial line for both dipoles.

so $\vec{E}_1=\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i}$ and $\overrightarrow{E_2}=\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i}$

So

$$ \begin{aligned} \vec{E} & =\vec{E}_1+\vec{E}_2 \\ & =\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i}+\frac{2 k p}{r^3} \hat{i} \\ \Rightarrow \vec{E} & =\frac{4 k p}{r^3} \hat{i} \\ \Rightarrow \vec{E} & =4\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\right) \frac{p}{r^3} \hat{i} \\ \Rightarrow \vec{E} & =\frac{p}{\pi \varepsilon_0 r^3} \hat{i} \end{aligned} $$

So $(S) \rightarrow(5)$

Hence, option (c) is correct.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: Two co-axial conducting cylinders of same length $\ell$ with radii $\sqrt{2}R$ and $2R$ are kept, as shown in Fig. 1. The charge on the inner cylinder is $Q$ and the outer cylinder is grounded. The annular region between the cylinders is filled with a material of dielectric constant $\kappa=5$. Consider an imaginary plane of the same length $\ell$ at a distance $R$ from the common axis of the cylinders. This plane is parallel to the axis of the cylinders. The cross-sectional view of this arrangement is shown in Fig. 2. Ignoring edge effects, the flux of the electric field through the plane is ($\epsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space):
questions.solution:
Here we are assuming that " $\ell$ " is very large just for the sake of symmetry. Outside cylinder will have zero electric field inside, so the flux generated on the plate will be due to inner cylinder only in sections AB and CD , as section be will be at that place where electric field is zero.

Flux through element will be =

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{d} \phi & =\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}} \\ \mathrm{~d} \phi & =\frac{2 \mathrm{k} \lambda}{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{dy} \cdot \ell \cdot \cos \theta .........(1) \end{aligned} $$

from figure we can say that

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cos \theta=\frac{R}{r} \Rightarrow r=R \sec \theta \\ & \tan \theta=\frac{y}{R} \Rightarrow y=R \tan \theta \\ & \Rightarrow d y=R \sec ^2 \theta d \theta \\ & d \phi=\frac{2 k \lambda}{R \sec \theta} R \cdot \sec ^2 \theta \cdot \ell \cdot \cos \theta d \theta \\ & d \phi=2 k \lambda \ell d q \end{aligned} $$

$\begin{aligned} & \int_0^{\phi_{\mathrm{AB}}} \mathrm{d} \phi=2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \ell \int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 3} \mathrm{~d} \theta \\ & \phi_{\mathrm{AB}}=2 \mathrm{k} \lambda \ell\left[\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right] \\ & \phi_{\mathrm{AB}}=2 \mathrm{kQ}\left[\frac{\pi}{12}\right] \\ & \phi_{\mathrm{AB}}=2 \times \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 \epsilon_{\mathrm{r}}} \mathrm{Q}\left[\frac{\pi}{12}\right] \\ & \phi_{\mathrm{AB}}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{120 \epsilon_0} \\ & \phi_{\text {plate }}=\phi_{\mathrm{AB}}+\phi_{\mathrm{BC}}+\phi_{\mathrm{CD}} \quad\left(\phi_{\mathrm{CD}}=\phi_{\mathrm{AB}}\right) \\ & \phi_{\text {plate }}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{60 \epsilon_0}\end{aligned}$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: A positive point charge of $10^{-8}$ C is kept at a distance of 20 cm from the center of a neutral conducting sphere of radius 10 cm. The sphere is then grounded and the charge on the sphere is measured. The grounding is then removed and subsequently the point charge is moved by a distance of 10 cm further away from the center of the sphere along the radial direction. Taking $\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9$ Nm$^2$/C$^2$ (where $\epsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space), which of the following statements is/are correct:
options: Before the grounding, the electrostatic potential of the sphere is 450 V.
Charge flowing from the sphere to the ground because of grounding is $5 \times 10^{-9}$ C.
After the grounding is removed, the charge on the sphere is $-5 \times 10^{-9}$ C.
The final electrostatic potential of the sphere is 300 V.
questions.solution:
Before grounding

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{V}_{\text {sphere }}=\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)_{\text {net }}=\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)_{\mathrm{q}}+\left(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}\right)_{\text {ind }} \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\text {sphere }}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\ell}+0=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-8}}{0.2}=\frac{90}{0.2}=\frac{900}{2}=450 \text { volt } \end{aligned} $$

After grounding

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{kQ}}{\ell}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\mathrm{V}_{\text {sphere }}^{\prime}=0 \\ & \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{s}}=-\frac{\mathrm{R}}{\ell} \mathrm{q}=-\frac{1}{2} \times 10^{-8}=-5 \times 10^{-9} \\ & \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{s}}=-5 \times 10^{-9} \text { Coulomb } \end{aligned} $$

Charge flower from sphere to ground $=5 \times 10^{-9}$ Coulomb

After grounding is removed

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {sphere }}\right)_{\text {final }}=\frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\ell^{\prime}}+\frac{\mathrm{kq}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10^2 \times 10^{-8}}{30}-\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^2}{10} \\ & =\frac{9 \times 1000}{30}-450=300 \mathrm{volt}-450 \mathrm{volt}=-150 \mathrm{volt} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Six infinitely large and thin non-conducting sheets are fixed in configurations I and II. As shown in the figure, the sheets carry uniform surface charge densities which are indicated in terms of $\sigma_0$. The separation between any two consecutive sheets is $1~\mu \text{m}$. The various regions between the sheets are denoted as 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5. If $\sigma_0 = 9~\mu\text{C/m}^2$, then which of the following statements is/are correct:

(Take permittivity of free space $\epsilon_0 = 9 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
options: In region 4 of the configuration I, the magnitude of the electric field is zero.
In region 3 of the configuration II, the magnitude of the electric field is $\dfrac{\sigma_0}{\epsilon_0}$.
Potential difference between the first and the last sheets of the configuration I is 5 V.
Potential difference between the first and the last sheets of the configuration II is zero.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}\left(E_4\right)_I= & \frac{\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[1-1+1-1-1+1]=0 \\ \left(V_{\text {First }}\right)_I & =\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[-1+2-3+4-5] \mathrm{d} \\ & =\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[-3] \mathrm{d}=\frac{\sigma_0 3 \mathrm{~d}}{2 \epsilon_0}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned}\left(V_{\text {Last }}\right)_I & =\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[1-2+3-4+5] \\ & =\frac{\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[-3 \mathrm{~d}]\end{aligned} \\ & \begin{aligned}\left(V_{\text {First }}-V_{\text {Last }}\right)_I & =\frac{3 \sigma_0 \mathrm{~d}}{\epsilon_0} \\ & =\frac{3 \times 9 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{-12}}=3 \mathrm{volt}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned}\left(\mathrm{E}_3\right)_{\text {II }}= & \frac{\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}\left[\frac{1}{2}-1+1+1-1+\frac{1}{2}\right]=\frac{\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0} \\ & =\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}\left[-1+2-3+4-\frac{5}{2}\right] \mathrm{d} \\ & =\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[2-2.5] \mathrm{d}=\frac{\sigma_0 \mathrm{~d}}{4 \epsilon_0}\end{aligned} \\ & \left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {Last }}\right)_{\text {II }}=\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}\left[1-2+3-4+\frac{5}{2}\right] \mathrm{d} \\ & \\ & \quad=\frac{-\sigma_0}{2 \epsilon_0}[6.5-6] \mathrm{d}=\frac{-\sigma_0 \mathrm{~d}}{4 \epsilon_0} \\ & \left(\mathrm{~V}_{\text {First }}-\mathrm{V}_{\text {Last }}\right)_{\text {II }}=\frac{\sigma_0 \mathrm{~d}}{2 \epsilon_0} \neq 0\end{aligned}$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: Tangents are drawn to the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ at points where it is intersected by the line $l x+m y+n=0$. The point of intersection of tangents at these points is
options:
$\left(\frac{a l}{n}, \frac{b m}{n}\right)$

$\left(\frac{a^2 l}{m}, \frac{b^2 m}{n}\right)$

$\left(\frac{b l}{n}, \frac{a m}{n}\right)$

$\left(\frac{-a^2 l}{n}, \frac{-b^2 m}{n}\right)$
questions.solution:
Let $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ be the point of intersection of tangents to the ellipse, where it is intersected by the line $l x+m y+n=0$. Then, the equation of the chord of contact of tangents is

$$ \frac{x x_1}{a^2}+\frac{y y_1}{b^2}=1\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i) $$

Clearly, $l x+m y+n=0$ and (i) represents the same line.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{x_1}{a^2 l}=\frac{y_1}{b^2 m}=\frac{1}{-n} \\ \Rightarrow & x_1=\frac{-a^2 l}{n}, y_1=\frac{-b^2 m}{n} \end{array} $$

Thus, the required point is $\left(\frac{-a^2 l}{n}, \frac{-b^2 m}{n}\right)$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question:
A rectangle is inscribed in an ellipse with the equation $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$

What is the maximum area of the rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse?
questions.solution:
Let the rectangle have vertices at $( \pm x, \pm y)$ on the ellipse. From the ellipse equation

$$ x=a \cos \theta, y=b \sin \theta $$

The area of the rectangle is

$$ A=4 x y=4(a \cos \theta \cdot b \sin \theta) $$

Using the identity $\sin 2 \theta=2 \sin \theta \cos \theta$, we rewrite $A=2 a b \sin 2 \theta$

To maximize $A$, we maximize $\sin 2 \theta$, which reaches its maximum value of 1 when $2 \theta=\frac{\pi}{2}$.

Thus, the maximum area is

$$ A_{\max }=2 a b $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let $P\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ and $Q\left(x_2, y_2\right)$ be two distinct points on the ellipse

$$ \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1 $$

such that $y_1>0$, and $y_2>0$. Let $C$ denote the circle $x^2+y^2=9$, and $M$ be the point $(3,0)$.

Suppose the line $x=x_1$ intersects $C$ at $R$, and the line $x=x_2$ intersects C at $S$, such that the $y$-coordinates of $R$ and $S$ are positive. Let $\angle R O M=\frac{\pi}{6}$ and $\angle S O M=\frac{\pi}{3}$, where $O$ denotes the origin $(0,0)$. Let $|X Y|$ denote the length of the line segment $X Y$.

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options: The equation of the line joining P and Q is $2x + 3y = 3(1 + \sqrt{3})$
The equation of the line joining P and Q is $2x + y = 3(1 + \sqrt{3})$
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{P} \equiv\left(3 \cos 30^{\circ}, 2 \sin 30^{\circ}\right) \equiv\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}, 1\right) \\ & \mathrm{Q} \equiv\left(3 \cos 60^{\circ}, 2 \sin 60^{\circ}\right) \equiv\left(\frac{3}{2}, \sqrt{3}\right) \\ & \mathrm{R}\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{3}{2}\right), \mathrm{S}\left(\frac{3}{2}, \frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\end{aligned}$

Slope of $\mathrm{PQ}=\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}-1}{\frac{3}{2}-\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}}=-\frac{2}{3}$

Equation of line PQ

$$ y-\sqrt{3}=-\frac{2}{3}\left(x-\frac{3}{2}\right) $$

$\Rightarrow 2 x+3 y=3(\sqrt{3}+1) \quad$ option (A) is correct

Now

if $\mathrm{N}_2=\left(\mathrm{x}_2, 0\right)=\left(\frac{3}{2}, 0\right)$

$\left|\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{Q}\right|=\sqrt{3}$ and $\left|\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right|=\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}$

$\Rightarrow 3\left|\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{Q}\right|=2\left|\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{~S}\right| \quad$ option (C) is correct

Now, if $\mathrm{N}_1=\left(\mathrm{x}_1, 0\right) \Rightarrow \mathrm{N}_1=\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{3}}{2}, 0\right)$

$\Rightarrow\left|\mathrm{N}_1 \mathrm{P}\right|=1,\left|\mathrm{~N}_1 \mathrm{R}\right|=\frac{3}{2} \quad$ option (D) is incorrect

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If any point on an ellipse is (3sin$\alpha$, 5cos$\alpha$), then what is the eccentricity of the ellipse?
questions.solution:
1. Given point $(x,y)=(3\sin\alpha,\;5\cos\alpha)$.

  2. Then

    $\sin\alpha=\frac{x}{3},\quad \cos\alpha=\frac{y}{5}.$

  3. Using $\sin^2\alpha+\cos^2\alpha=1$,

    $$ \Bigl(\frac{x}{3}\Bigr)^2+\Bigl(\frac{y}{5}\Bigr)^2=1. $$

  4. This is the ellipse with semi-major axis $a=5$ and semi-minor axis $b=3$.

  5. Eccentricity $e=\sqrt{1-\frac{b^2}{a^2}} =\sqrt{1-\frac{3^2}{5^2}} =\sqrt{\frac{16}{25}} =\frac{4}{5}.$

Answer: $\displaystyle \frac{4}{5}$ (Option B).

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A large block of ice 10 cm thick with a vertical hole drilled through it is floating in a lake. The minimum length of the rope required to scoop out a bucket full of water through the hole is (density of ice $=0.9 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ )
questions.solution:
In equilibrium,weight $=$ upthrust

i.e.  $$ V \rho_i g=V_i \rho_w g $$

or  $$ \frac{V_i}{V}=\frac{\rho_i}{\rho_w}=\frac{0.9}{1.0}=0.9 $$

i.e. $90 \%$ or 9 m of ice block is inside the water. Therefore, minimum length $l$ required to scoop out a bucket of water is $10-9=1.0 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A water film is made between two straight parallel wires of length 10 cm each and at a distance of 0.5 cm from each other. If the distance between the wires is increased by 1 mm , then work done (in ergs) is [Given, surface tension of water $=72$ dynes $/ \mathrm{cm}$ ]
questions.solution:
Force due to surface tension of the film on the wire,

As, there are two free surfaces,

$$ F=2 \times T \times 10 \mathrm{~cm} $$

∴ Work done in increasing the distance by 1 mm ,

$$ \begin{aligned} W & =F \times 0.1 \\ & =2 \times T \times 10(0.1) \\ & =2 \times 72 \times 10 \times 0.1=144 \mathrm{ergs} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: If $f: X \rightarrow Y$ be a function defined by $f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c$ and $f$ is bijective, then the set $X$ with $\theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & f(x)=a \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)+b \cos x+c \\ & f(x)=\frac{a \sin x}{\sqrt{2}}+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right) \cos x+c=r \sin (x+\theta)+c \\ & \text { where } r=\sqrt{\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2+\left(\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}+b\right)^2} \text { and } \\ & \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

For $f$ to be one-one, we must have

$$ \begin{aligned} & -\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x+\theta \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta \Rightarrow x \in\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right] \end{aligned} $$

Hence, $X=\left[\frac{-\pi}{2}-\theta, \frac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right]$, where

$$ \theta=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{a+\sqrt{2} b}{a}\right) $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: If the function $f: R \rightarrow R$ is defined by $f(x)=x^2+5 x+9$, then $f^{-1}(9)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } f^{-1}(9)=x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f(x)=9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+5 x+9=9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^2+5 x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x(x+5)=0 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & x & =0,-5 \text { which are in } R . \\ \therefore & & f^{-1}(9) & =\{0,-5\} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let ℕ denote the set of all natural numbers, and ℤ denote the set of all integers. Consider the functions f: ℕ → ℤ and g: ℤ → ℕ defined by

$$ f(n) = \begin{cases} \frac{(n + 1)}{2} & \text{if } n \text{ is odd,} \\ \frac{(4-n)}{2} & \text{if } n \text{ is even,} \end{cases} $$

and

$$ g(n) = \begin{cases} 3 + 2n & \text{if } n \ge 0 , \\ -2n & \text{if } n < 0 . \end{cases} $$

Define $$(g \circ f)(n) = g(f(n))$$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$, and $$(f \circ g)(n) = f(g(n))$$ for all $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
options: g $\circ $ f is NOT one-one and g $\circ $ f is NOT onto
f $\circ $ g is NOT one-one but f $\circ $ g is onto

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let ℝ denote the set of all real numbers. Let f: ℝ → ℝ be a function such that f(x) > 0 for all x ∈ ℝ, and f(x+y) = f(x)f(y) for all x, y ∈ ℝ.

Let the real numbers a₁, a₂, ..., a₅₀ be in an arithmetic progression. If f(a₃₁) = 64f(a₂₅), and

$ \sum\limits_{i=1}^{50} f(a_i) = 3(2^{25}+1), $

then the value of

$ \sum\limits_{i=6}^{30} f(a_i) $

is ________________.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let $\mathbb{R}$ denote the set of all real numbers. Let $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ and $g: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow(0,4)$ be functions defined by

$$ f(x)=\log _e\left(x^2+2 x+4\right), \text { and } g(x)=\frac{4}{1+e^{-2 x}} $$

Define the composite function $f \circ g^{-1}$ by $\left(f \circ g^{-1}\right)(x)=f\left(g^{-1}(x)\right)$, where $g^{-1}$ is the inverse of the function $g$.

Then the value of the derivative of the composite function $f \circ g^{-1}$ at $x=2$ is ________________.
questions.solution:
Function $ f $:

$ f(x) = \log_e\left(x^2 + 2x + 4\right) = \log_e\left[(x+1)^2 + 3\right] $

Function $ g $:

$ g(x) = \frac{4}{1 + e^{-2x}} $

To find the inverse of $ g $, $ g^{-1} $, we solve for $ x $:

Start with $ y = \frac{4}{1 + e^{-2x}} $.

Rearranging gives $ 1 + e^{-2x} = \frac{4}{y} $.

Solving for $ e^{-2x} $, we have $ e^{-2x} = \frac{4}{y} - 1 $.

Taking the natural logarithm, we find $ -2x = \ln\left(\frac{4}{y} - 1\right) $.

Thus, we solve for $ x $:

$ x = -\frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{4-y}{y}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{y}{4-y}\right) $

So, the inverse function:

$ g^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{x}{4-x}\right) $

Now, let's evaluate $ g^{-1}(2) $:

$ g^{-1}(2) = \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{2}{4-2}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \ln(1) = 0 $

Thus, $ f(g^{-1}(x)) = f\left(\frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{x}{4-x}\right)\right) $.

Next, we differentiate $ f \circ g^{-1} $:

$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(f(g^{-1}(x))\right) = \frac{d}{dx}\left[\log_e\left(\left(g^{-1}(x) + 1\right)^2 + 3\right)\right] $

Using the chain rule, the derivative is:

$ \frac{1}{\left[\left(g^{-1}(x) + 1\right)^2 + 3\right]} \cdot 2 \left(g^{-1}(x) + 1\right) \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(g^{-1}(x)\right) $

Calculate the derivative of $ g^{-1}(x) $:

$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{x}{4-x}\right)\right) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\frac{x}{4-x}} \cdot \left(\frac{(4-x)x' + x(4-x)'}{(4-x)^2}\right) $

Evaluate at $ x = 2 $:

$ g^{-1}(2) = 0 $ means $ \left(g^{-1}(2) + 1\right)^2 + 3 = 4 $.

At $ x = 2 $:

$ \frac{d}{dx} f(g^{-1}(x))\big|_{x=2} = \frac{2}{4} \cdot \frac{2}{4} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 $

Therefore, the value of the derivative of the composite function $ f \circ g^{-1} $ at $ x = 2 $ is $ 0.25 $.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the range of the function?
options: (−∞,0.5]
questions.solution:
cos x varies between –1 and 1, so

$$1 - \cos x\in[\,0,2\,].$$

But since $x\ne2n\pi$, $\cos x\ne1$, hence

$$1 - \cos x\in(0,2].$$

Therefore

$$y=\frac1{1-\cos x}\in\Bigl[\frac1{2},\infty\Bigr).$$

 • Minimum occurs when $\cos x=-1$ (i.e.\ $x=(2n+1)\pi$), giving $y=\tfrac12$.

 • As $\cos x\to1$, $1-\cos x\to0^+$ so $y\to+\infty$.

Answer: Option B $[0.5,\infty)$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the domain of the function (f(x))?
questions.solution:
Domain of a function is the set of all real $x$ for which the formula makes sense. Here,

$$f(x)=|x-3|$$

involves the expression $(x-3)$, which is a polynomial and is defined for every real number. Therefore:

Inside the absolute value we have $x-3$, and there is no division by zero or square root of a negative—no restriction.

Hence $x$ can be any real number.

So the domain is

$$(-\infty,\;\infty)$$

which corresponds to Option C.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If f(x) = px + q then what is the value of (p + q) ?
questions.solution:
We know the graph of a linear function $f(x)=px+q$ passes through $(1,1)$ and $(2,4)$.

Substitute $(1,1)$:

$ p\cdot1 + q = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p + q = 1 \quad\text{(Equation 1)} $

Substitute $(2,4)$:

$ p\cdot2 + q = 4 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 2p + q = 4 \quad\text{(Equation 2)} $

Subtract Equation 1 from Equation 2:

$ (2p+q)-(p+q)=4-1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad p = 3 $

Put $p=3$ into $p+q=1$:

$ 3 + q = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad q = -2 $

Finally,

$ p+q = 3 + (-2) = 1. $

Answer: Option C (1).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is f(16) equal to?
questions.solution:
Find f(1):

Set y=1 in the given relation

$$f\!\bigl(\tfrac{x}{1}\bigr)=\frac{f(x)}{f(1)}$$

But $f(x/1)=f(x)$. So

$$f(x)=\frac{f(x)}{f(1)}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(1)=1.$$

Show f is multiplicative:

For any positive $a,b$, let $x=ab$ and $y=b$. Then

$$f\!\bigl(\tfrac{ab}{b}\bigr)=\frac{f(ab)}{f(b)}$$

i.e.

$$f(a)=\frac{f(ab)}{f(b)}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(ab)=f(a)\,f(b).$$

General form of f:

A positive, multiplicative function satisfying $f(1)=1$ is

$$f(x)=x^k$$

for some constant $k$.

Determine k using f(2)=3:

$$f(2)=2^k=3\quad\Longrightarrow\quad k=\frac{\ln 3}{\ln 2}.$$

Compute f(16):

$$f(16)=16^k=\bigl(2^4\bigr)^k=2^{4k}=\bigl(2^k\bigr)^4=3^4=81.$$

Answer: Option D (81).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is f(1)f(4) equal to?
questions.solution:
From the given relation

$$f\biggl(\frac{x}{y}\biggr)=\frac{f(x)}{f(y)}$$

set $x=y$. Then

$$f(1)=\frac{f(y)}{f(y)}=1.$$

To show multiplicativity, let $a=xy$ and $b=y$. Then $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{xy}{y}=x$, so

$$f(x)=f\Bigl(\frac{a}{b}\Bigr)=\frac{f(a)}{f(b)} \;\Longrightarrow\;f(a)=f(b)\,f(x)\,. $$

That is, for all positive $x,y$,

$$f(xy)=f(x)\,f(y)\,. $$

Now, since $f(2)=3$, we get

$$f(4)=f(2\cdot2)=f(2)\,f(2)=3\cdot3=9.$$

Finally,

$$f(1)\,f(4)=1\cdot9=9.$$

Answer: Option C (9).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is f(0) equal to?
questions.solution:
Let $x=0$. Then the given relation

$ f(xy)=f(x+y) $

becomes

$ f(0\cdot y)=f(0+y)\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(0)=f(y)\quad\text{for all real }y. $

This shows $f$ is a constant function.

Since $f$ is constant and $f(5)=10$, it follows that for every input, including $0$,

$ f(0)=10. $

Answer: Option D (10).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is f(20)+f(-20) equal to?
questions.solution:
We want to find f(20). By the given property, if we can find real x,y such that

• xy = 5

• x+y = 20

then

$$f(20)=f(x+y)=f(xy)=f(5)=10\,. $$

Solve for x (or y):

$$x+\frac{5}{x}=20\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^2-20x+5=0\,. $$

Discriminant

$$\Delta=20^2-4\cdot1\cdot5=400-20=380>0$$

so there are real solutions. Hence such real x,y exist and

$$f(20)=10\,. $$

Similarly for f(−20), find real x,y with

• xy = 5

• x+y = −20

$$x+\frac{5}{x}=-20\quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^2+20x+5=0\,, $$

$$\Delta=20^2-4\cdot1\cdot5=400-20=380>0\,. $$

So real solutions exist, giving

$$f(-20)=f(x+y)=f(xy)=f(5)=10\,. $$

Therefore

$$f(20)+f(-20)=10+10=20\,. $$

Answer: Option C (20).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is $\frac{f(x)}{f(x+1)}$ equal to?
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=\frac{x}{1-x}, \qquad x>0,\ x\ne 1 $$

We need to find

$$ \frac{f(x)}{f(x+1)} $$

First, write both terms separately.

$$ f(x)=\frac{x}{1-x} $$

Now,

$$ f(x+1)=\frac{x+1}{1-(x+1)} $$

Simplify the denominator:

$$ 1-(x+1)=1-x-1=-x $$

So,

$$ f(x+1)=\frac{x+1}{-x}=-\frac{x+1}{x} $$

Now compute the ratio:

$$ \frac{f(x)}{f(x+1)} = \frac{\frac{x}{1-x}}{-\frac{x+1}{x}} $$

Divide by a fraction by multiplying with its reciprocal:

$$ = \frac{x}{1-x}\cdot \frac{x}{-(x+1)} $$

$$ = -\frac{x^2}{(1-x)(x+1)} $$

Since

$$ (1-x)(x+1)=1-x^2 $$

we get

$$ \frac{f(x)}{f(x+1)}=-\frac{x^2}{1-x^2} $$

Now compare this with

$$ f(x^2)=\frac{x^2}{1-x^2} $$

Therefore,

$$ \frac{f(x)}{f(x+1)}=-f(x^2) $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1: }-f(x^2)} $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is $ (1-x)f(\sqrt{x})+xf(\sqrt{x}+1)$ equal to?
questions.solution:
$ f(x)=\frac{x}{1-x}, \qquad x>0,\ x\ne 1 $

We have to find

$ (1-x)f(\sqrt{x})+x f(\sqrt{x}+1) $

Let $t=\sqrt{x}$. Then $x=t^2$.

Now,

$ f(\sqrt{x})=f(t)=\frac{t}{1-t} $

and

$ f(\sqrt{x}+1)=f(t+1)=\frac{t+1}{1-(t+1)}=\frac{t+1}{-t}=-\frac{t+1}{t} $

Substitute these into the expression:

$ (1-x)f(\sqrt{x})+x f(\sqrt{x}+1) $

$ =(1-t^2)\cdot \frac{t}{1-t}+t^2\left(-\frac{t+1}{t}\right) $

Now simplify step by step.

Since

$ 1-t^2=(1-t)(1+t), $

so

$ (1-t^2)\cdot \frac{t}{1-t}=(1+t)t $

Also,

$ t^2\left(-\frac{t+1}{t}\right)=-t(t+1) $

Therefore,

$ (1+t)t - t(t+1)=0 $

Hence,

$ (1-x)f(\sqrt{x})+x f(\sqrt{x}+1)=0 $

So the correct option is:

$ \boxed{0} $

Option 4.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is the domain of the function?
questions.solution:
We need the domain of

$$ f(x)=1-\sqrt[3]{(x-1)^2}. $$

To find the domain, we check for any restriction on $x$.

Here,

$$ (x-1)^2 $$

is defined for every real number $x$.

Also, the cube root

$$ \sqrt[3]{\,\cdot\,} $$

is defined for all real numbers, unlike a square root which needs a non-negative number.

Since $(x-1)^2 \ge 0$ for every real $x$, its cube root is certainly defined for every real $x$.

So there is no restriction on $x$.

Therefore, the domain is

$$ (-\infty,\infty). $$

So the correct option is:

Option 2: $(-\infty,\infty)$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is$f\circ f\circ f\circ f\circ f(0)$ equal to?
questions.solution:
We need to find

$$ f\circ f\circ f\circ f\circ f(0)=f(f(f(f(f(0))))) $$

given

$$ f(x)=\frac{10^x-10^{-x}}{10^x+10^{-x}} $$

First, find $f(0)$.

Since

$$ 10^0=1 \quad \text{and} \quad 10^{-0}=1 $$

so

$$ f(0)=\frac{1-1}{1+1}=\frac{0}{2}=0 $$

Now apply $f$ again:

$$ f(f(0))=f(0)=0 $$

Similarly,

$$ f(f(f(0)))=f(0)=0 $$

and every time we apply $f$ to $0$, we get $0$ again.

Therefore,

$$ f\circ f\circ f\circ f\circ f(0)=0 $$

So the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{0} $$

Option 1.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: What is the inverse of the function?
questions.solution:
Given

$$ f(x)=\frac{10^x-10^{-x}}{10^x+10^{-x}} $$

We have to find $f^{-1}(x)$.

Let

$$ y=\frac{10^x-10^{-x}}{10^x+10^{-x}} $$

Now solve for $x$ in terms of $y$.

Multiply both sides:

$$ y(10^x+10^{-x})=10^x-10^{-x} $$

Expand:

$$ y10^x+y10^{-x}=10^x-10^{-x} $$

Bring like terms together:

$$ 10^x-y10^x=y10^{-x}+10^{-x} $$

Take common factors:

$$ 10^x(1-y)=10^{-x}(1+y) $$

Now divide by $10^{-x}$:

$$ 10^{2x}(1-y)=1+y $$

So,

$$ 10^{2x}=\frac{1+y}{1-y} $$

Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$ 2x=\log_{10}\left(\frac{1+y}{1-y}\right) $$

Hence,

$$ x=\frac{1}{2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{1+y}{1-y}\right) $$

Therefore,

$$ f^{-1}(x)=\frac{1}{2}\log_{10}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right) $$

So the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 4}} $$

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements with regard to a steel plant:
I. It was setup in 1964 with Russian collaboration
II. It receives iron ore from the Rourkela region
III. Water and Hydel power is supplied by the Damodar Valley Corporation
Identify the Steel Plant on the basis of the above facts:
questions.solution:
The correct steel plant, based on the provided facts, is the Bhilai Steel Plant.

Key Points

Bhilai Steel Plant: Established in 1955 through collaboration with the Soviet Union, it is located in Chhattisgarh.

Iron Ore Supply: This plant sources its iron ore primarily from the Dalli-Rajhara mines, not the Rourkela region.

Product Specialization: It is renowned for producing rails for the Indian Railways along with a variety of steel products.

Energy Supply: The Damodar Valley Corporation provides water and hydel power to the plant.

Impact: Bhilai Steel Plant has significantly contributed to making India a self-reliant producer of steel and played a pivotal role in the country's industrial advancement.

Additional Information

Damodar Valley Corporation (DVC):

A government entity established in 1948.

Provides hydel and thermal power to eastern Indian industries and municipalities.

Manages multiple dams, reservoirs, and power plants in the Damodar River basin.

Steel Plants in India:

Major steel plants include Bhilai, Bokaro, Rourkela, and Durgapur.

Each plant is known for distinctive collaborations and specific sources for raw materials and energy.

Russian Collaboration in Indian Steel Industry:

The Soviet Union significantly contributed to India's steel industry, including the Bhilai and Bokaro plants.

Marked a landmark in Indo-Soviet relations during the mid-20th century.

Indian Steel Industry Overview:

India stands as one of the largest steel producers globally, with a comprehensive network of integrated and mini steel plants.

The industry is vital for infrastructure development and economic growth.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Gaja Dwar, Ashwa Dwar, Garuda Dwar, Makar Dwar, Shardula Dwar and Hamsa Dwar are located in a building at
questions.solution:
The monumental gates Gaja Dwar, Ashwa Dwar, Garuda Dwar, Makar Dwar, Shardula Dwar, and Hamsa Dwar are located at the Rashtrapati Bhavan in New Delhi.

Key Points

Monumental Gates: These gates are significant entrances to the Rashtrapati Bhavan, the official residence of the President of India, and are architecturally significant, representing different symbolic animals.

Architectural Heritage: Designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker, Rashtrapati Bhavan is an icon of British colonial architecture, inaugurated in 1931. The gates symbolize the heritage and cultural motifs of India.

Ceremonial Function: Besides their architectural beauty, these gates serve ceremonial purposes, underlining the historical importance of Rashtrapati Bhavan.

Additional Information

Rashtrapati Bhavan:

Scale and Function: It spans 130 hectares, making it one of the largest residences of a head of state in the world, containing 340 rooms, including official spaces for the President, guest accommodations, and ceremonial areas.

Mughal Gardens: Known for their beauty, these gardens are open to the public during the annual "Udyanotsav."

Architectural Style:

Design: The building combines classical European and Indian architectural styles. The dome is inspired by the Sanchi Stupa, with elements reflecting Indian traditions.

Ceremonial Gates:

Symbolism: Each gate, named after an animal, embodies qualities such as strength and wisdom, integral to Indian culture. The gates are adorned with intricate motifs and carvings enhancing their aesthetic value.

Sir Edwin Lutyens:

Legacy: As the chief architect of New Delhi, Lutyens designed numerous landmark structures, including the Rashtrapati Bhavan. The term "Lutyens' Delhi" denotes the area showcasing his architectural influence.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which dance form of India has been inscribed into UNESCO’s Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Garba.

Key Points

Garba, a traditional dance from Gujarat, India, was added to UNESCO's Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity in 2022.

It is primarily performed during the Navratri festival, which honors Goddess Durga and celebrates the triumph of good over evil.

The dance symbolizes devotion, community bonding, and festivity, typically performed in circles around a lamp or an image of the goddess.

Garba's recognition by UNESCO underscores its cultural importance and helps in preserving and promoting India's intangible heritage worldwide.

Additional Information

UNESCO Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage:

The purpose of this list is to protect cultural traditions and practices that communities consider part of their heritage.

India has several elements on this list, including Yoga, Kumbh Mela, and Garba.

Garba vs. Dandiya Raas:

Garba uses hand and foot movements, whereas Dandiya incorporates sticks.

Both are popular during Navratri but vary in technique and presentation.

Navratri Festival:

A nine-day festival dedicated to Goddess Durga, celebrated with great enthusiasm across India, particularly in Gujarat.

Garba is a key component of Navratri festivities.

India and Intangible Cultural Heritage:

India boasts a rich tradition of diverse art forms, practices, and festivals recognized globally.

There are ongoing efforts to document and preserve these traditions for future generations.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: India’s Gas Turbine Research Establishment (GTRE), part of the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) has developed an aero-engine which will potentially make India self-reliance in aero-engine technology. What is the name of the engine?
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Kaveri.

Key Points

The Kaveri engine is an indigenous aero-engine developed by the Gas Turbine Research Establishment (GTRE), a laboratory under the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO).

It was specifically designed for India's Light Combat Aircraft (LCA) Tejas to achieve self-reliance in aviation propulsion systems.

The Kaveri engine project was initiated in the 1980s as part of India's efforts to reduce dependence on foreign suppliers for military equipment.

Although the Kaveri engine faced delays and challenges, its development has significantly contributed to India's expertise and technological capabilities in aero-engine manufacturing.

The engine has a thrust rating of approximately 80 kilonewtons, suitable for lightweight fighter jets like the LCA Tejas.

Additional Information

Gas Turbine Research Establishment (GTRE)

GTRE, established in 1959, is a DRDO laboratory specializing in gas turbine engine development for military and civil applications.

It plays a pivotal role in designing and testing advanced propulsion systems for India's aerospace sector.

Challenges in Aero-Engine Development

Aero-engine development involves complex requirements such as advanced metallurgy, thermal management, and precision engineering.

India faced challenges in sourcing advanced materials and overcoming technological gaps during the Kaveri project.

Light Combat Aircraft (LCA) Tejas

The Tejas is a multi-role lightweight fighter aircraft developed by Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL) for the Indian Air Force and Navy.

While initially intended to be powered by the Kaveri engine, the Tejas was equipped with the GE F404 engine due to delays in the Kaveri program.

Self-Reliance in Defence

The Kaveri engine project aligns with India's broader goal of achieving self-reliance in defence technology, complementing initiatives like "Make in India."

Despite the delays, the program has enriched India's knowledge base and will be valuable for future indigenous engine development.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which among the following agencies releases report on ‘S.A.F.E. Accommodation: Worker Housing for Manufacturing Growth’?
questions.solution:
The report on ‘S.A.F.E. Accommodation: Worker Housing for Manufacturing Growth’ is released by the Housing and Urban Development Corporation (HUDCO).

Key Points

About HUDCO: HUDCO is a public sector organization under the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, Government of India. It is dedicated to promoting sustainable housing and urban development through both financial and technical support.

Purpose of the Report: The report focuses on delivering safe and affordable housing for industrial and manufacturing workers. This aligns with government efforts to enhance worker welfare and stimulate economic growth by improving living standards in urban and industrial settings.

HUDCO's Contributions: HUDCO has been involved in various studies and reports aimed at tackling housing challenges and advancing urban infrastructure development in India.

Additional Information

HUDCO Overview:

Founded in 1970, HUDCO specializes in financing housing and urban infrastructure projects across India.

It operates under the Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs.

HUDCO also offers consultancy services for sustainable urban development projects.

Worker Housing Challenges:

Rapid urbanization often results in inadequate housing for industrial workers.

The absence of affordable and safe housing negatively affects productivity and well-being.

Government-supported initiatives, such as HUDCO's report, aim to systematically address these issues.

Affordable Housing Schemes in India:

The Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY) aims to provide affordable housing to both urban and rural poor.

State governments implement specific housing schemes that address local needs.

HUDCO collaborates with these schemes to ensure effective implementation and funding.

Sustainable Urban Development:

HUDCO encourages the use of green building technologies and eco-friendly construction practices.

The organization is committed to inclusive growth by addressing the housing and infrastructure needs of marginalized communities.

HUDCO's initiatives contribute to India's commitment to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

questions.title: NDA General Ability 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements with regard to National Technology Day :

1. It commemorates the momentous events of 11th May 1998, when India conducted successful nuclear tests under Operation Shakti

2. The official theme for National Technology Day 2025 is ‘School  to Startups — Igniting Young Minds to Innovate’

Which of the statements given above is/are correct ?
questions.solution:
Statement 1

"It commemorates the momentous events of 11th May 1998, when India conducted successful nuclear tests under Operation Shakti."

  • National Technology Day is observed every year on 11th May in India.

  • On this day in 1998, India successfully carried out nuclear tests at Pokhran, Rajasthan, known as Pokhran-II or Operation Shakti, under the leadership of then Prime Minister Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee.

  • On the same day, India also successfully test-fired the Hansa-3 aircraft and the Trishul missile.

  • Recognising these achievements, Shri Atal Bihari Vajpayee declared 11th May as National Technology Day.

Hence, Statement 1 is correct.

Statement 2

"The official theme for National Technology Day 2025 is 'School to Startups — Igniting Young Minds to Innovate.'"

  • The theme "From Schools to Startups: Igniting Young Minds to Innovate" was actually the theme for National Technology Day 2024, not 2025.

  • Therefore, the theme mentioned here has been wrongly attributed to the year 2025.

Hence, Statement 2 is incorrect.

Correct Answer

Option 1: 1 only

Quick Recap

  • Date: 11th May (every year)

  • First observed: 1999

  • Commemorates: Pokhran-II nuclear tests (Operation Shakti), Hansa-3 test flight, and Trishul missile test — all in 1998.

  • Nodal Agency: Technology Development Board (TDB), under the Department of Science and Technology.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following lines of Longitude and Latitude:
1.Prime Meridian
2.Tropic of Cancer
3.Equator
Which of the above lines is/are a Great Circle?
questions.solution:
Consider the following lines of longitude and latitude:

Prime Meridian

Tropic of Cancer

Equator

The correct Great Circle is 3 only, which refers to the Equator.

Key Points

A Great Circle is the largest possible circle on a sphere, dividing it into two equal halves.

The Equator is a Great Circle because it splits the Earth into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.

The Prime Meridian is not a Great Circle since it merely connects the North and South Poles, not dividing the Earth equally.

The Tropic of Cancer is not a Great Circle; it is a parallel located north of the Equator and does not split the Earth into equal halves.

Therefore, only the Equator is considered a Great Circle among the given options.

Additional Information

Great Circle Definition: Formed when a plane passes through the center of a sphere, creating the largest possible circle on its surface.

Examples of Great Circles: The Equator is the only latitude line that is a Great Circle. All longitudinal lines, like the Prime Meridian, form half of a Great Circle when paired with their opposite meridian.

Importance in Navigation: Great Circles are crucial in navigation and aviation for determining the shortest distance between two points on Earth.

Tropic of Cancer and Tropic of Capricorn: These are parallels of latitude at 23.5°N and 23.5°S, respectively, and are not Great Circles as they do not equally split the Earth.

Prime Meridian: This line of 0° longitude runs through Greenwich, England, forming only a semicircle by itself and hence is not a Great Circle.
