questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: A 1 cm segment of a wire lying along $x$-axis carries current of 0.5 A along $+x$ direction. A magnetic field $\vec{B}=(0.4 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}+(0.6 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{k}$ is switched on, in the region. The force acting on the segment is
questions.solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } I=0.5 \mathrm{~A}, l=1 \hat{i} \mathrm{~cm}=10^{-2} \hat{i}\,\,\, m, \\ & \vec{B}=(0.4 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{j}+(0.6 \mathrm{mT}) \hat{k} \\ & \vec{F}=I(\vec{l} \times \vec{B}) \\ & \vec{F}=0.5 \times 10^{-3}\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 10^{-2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.4 & 0.6 \end{array}\right| \\ & \vec{F}=0.5 \times 10^{-3}\left[-\left(0.6 \times 10^{-2}\right) \hat{j}+\left(0.4 \times 10^{-2}\right) \hat{k}\right] \\ & \vec{F}=0.5(-6 \hat{j}+4 \hat{k}) \mu \mathrm{N} \\ & \vec{F}=(-3 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}) \mu \mathrm{N} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: You are required to design an air-filled solenoid of inductance 0.016 H having a length 0.81 m and radius 0.02 m . The number of turns in the solenoid should be
questions.solution:
Given

  • Inductance $ L = 0.016 \, \text{H} $

  • Length $ l = 0.81 \, \text{m} $

  • Radius $ r = 0.02 \, \text{m} $

  • Air core (μ = μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m)

We need the number of turns N.

Formula for inductance of a solenoid

$ L = \mu_0 \frac{N^2 A}{l} $

where

$ A = \pi r^2 $ is the cross-sectional area.

Rearranging for $ N $:

$ N = \sqrt{\frac{L \, l}{\mu_0 \, A}} $

Substitute the values

$ A = \pi (0.02)^2 = \pi \times 4 \times 10^{-4} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2 $

$ \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} $

Now,

$ N^2 = \frac{L l}{\mu_0 A} = \frac{(0.016)(0.81)}{(1.2566\times10^{-6})(1.2566\times10^{-3})} $

Compute

$ L l = 0.01296 $

$ \mu_0 A = (1.2566\times10^{-6})(1.2566\times10^{-3}) = 1.579\times10^{-9} $

$ \frac{L l}{\mu_0 A} = \frac{0.01296}{1.579\times10^{-9}} \approx 8.21\times10^{6} $

Then,

$ N = \sqrt{8.21\times10^{6}} \approx 2866 $

✅ Answer: Option B — 2866 turns

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
Assertion (A) : The deflection in a galvanometer is directly proportional to the current passing through it.

Reason (R) : The coil of a galvanometer is suspended in a uniform radial magnetic field.
options: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason $(R)$ is the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of Assertion (A).
Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
Assertion (A) is false and Reason (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
Assertion (A): The statement is true because, in a galvanometer, the angle the needle moves ($\theta$) changes in direct proportion to the amount of electric current ($I$) going through it.

$$\theta \propto I$$

The reason for this is that the force (called torque) that turns the coil depends on the current. So, if the current increases, the coil turns more.

Reason (R): This statement is also true.

The coil in a galvanometer moves inside a magnetic field that is even and spread out evenly in all directions (this is called a uniform radial magnetic field). This setup makes sure that the force on the coil depends only on the current, not on the coil's position.

Because the magnetic field is set up this way, the mathematical part called “sine of the angle between coil and field” always has the same value. This means the force (torque) stays directly linked to the current.

How Reason (R) explains Assertion (A):

The uniform radial magnetic field is the key reason the needle's movement $\theta$ and the current $I$ stay in step (in a straight-line relationship). That’s why the deflection follows the rule $\theta \propto I$.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question:
(a) (i) A proton moving with velocity $\vec{V}$ in a non. uniform magnetic field traces a path as shown in the figure.

The path followed by the proton is always in the plane of the paper. What is the direction of the magnetic field in the region near points $P, Q$ and $R$ ? What can you say about relative magnitude of magnetic fields at these points?

(ii) A current carrying circular loop of area $A$ produces a magnetic field $B$ at its centre. Show that the magnetic moment of the loop is $\frac{2 B A}{\mu_0} \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}$

OR

(b) (i) Derive an expression for the torque acting on a rectangular current loop suspended in a uniform magnetic field.

(ii) A charged particle is moving in a circular path with velocity $\vec{V}$ in a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$. It is made to pass through a sheet of lead and as a consequence, it looses one half of its kinetic energy without change in its direction. How will (1) the radius of its path (2) its time period of revolution change?
questions.solution:
(a) (i) A proton is a positively charged particle, and its trajectory is influenced by the Lorentz force in a magnetic field. The force on a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ \vec{F}=q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) $$

Since, the force is always perpendicular to the velocity, the particle follows a curved path. The nature of curvature indicates the direction and variation in the magnetic field.

The proton follows a curved trajectory that remains in the plane of the paper.

This suggests that the force acting on the proton is perpendicular to its velocity in the plane.

Since, the proton curves upward, the force must be towards the centre of curvature, meaning the magnetic field is into the plane of the paper at all points using right-hand rule.

Thus, at points $P, Q$, and $R$, the magnetic field is directed into the paper.

The radius of curvature $r$ of a charged particle's motion in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ r=\frac{m v}{q B} $$

If the radius is larger, the magnetic field is weaker. If the radius is smaller, the magnetic field is stronger.

Observing the trajectory:

  • At $P$, the path is relatively straight, meaning $B$ is smaller.

  • At $Q$, the curvature increases, meaning $B$ is increasing.

  • At $R$, the curvature is highest, meaning $B$ is largest.

Thus, the relative magnitude follows: $B_P

(ii) The magnetic moment $(\mu)$ of a current-carrying circular loop is given by:

$$ \mu=I A $$

The magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop carrying current is given by:

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 R} $$

The area of the loop is:

$$ A=\pi R^2 \Rightarrow R=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}} $$

Now, substitute $R$ back into the equation for B :

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}} $$

This simplifies to: $B=\frac{\mu_0 I \sqrt{\pi}}{2 \sqrt{A}}$

Rearranging the equation to solve for $I$ :

$$ I=\frac{2 B \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}} $$

The magnetic moment $m$ of the loop is given by:

$$ m=I . A $$

Substituting the expression for $I$ :

$$ m=\left(\frac{2 B \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}}\right) \cdot A $$

This simplifies to: $m=\frac{2 B A \sqrt{A}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}}$

OR

(b) (i) Consider the simple case when a rectangular loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field $B$ that is in the plane of the loop.

Force on arm $A B=F_1=I b B$ (directed into the plane of the loop)

Force on arm $C D=F_2=I b B$ (directed outward the plane of the loop)

Therefore, the magnitude of the torque on the loop due to these pair of forces is

$$ \begin{aligned} \tau & =F_1 \frac{a}{2}+F_2 \frac{a}{2} \\ & =I(a b) B \\ & =I A B=m B \\ (A & =a b=\text { area of the loop }) \end{aligned} $$

Alternatively,

Also, accept if the student obtains the result.

$$ \text { Torque }=I A B \sin \theta $$

(ii) (1) The radius of a charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field is given by:

$$ r=\frac{m v}{q B} $$

The initial kinetic energy of the particle:

$$ K_i=\frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 $$

After losing half of its kinetic energy:

$$ \begin{aligned} K_f\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_f^2\right) & =\frac{1}{2} K_i=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v_i^2\right) \\ v_f & =\frac{v_i}{\sqrt{2}} \\ r & =\frac{m v}{q B} \\ r_f & =\frac{m v_f}{q B} \\ & =\frac{m v_i}{q B \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} r_i \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the radius decreases by a factor of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$.

(2) The time period of revolution for a charged particle in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ T=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B} $$

Since, the time period does not depend on velocity, it remains unchanged even though the kinetic energy is reduced.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics Delhi Set 1 - 2025
questions.question: Using Biot-Savart law, derive expression for the magnetic field $(B)$ due to a circular current carrying loop at a point on its axis and hence at its centre.
questions.solution:
Let us consider a circular loop of radius $a$ with centre C. Let the plane of the coil be perpendicular to plane of the paper and current $I$ be flowing in the direction shown. Suppose $P$ is any point on the axis a distance $r$ form the centre.

Let us consider a current element $d l$ on top $(L)$ where, current comes out of paper normally whereas at bottom ( $M$ ) enters into the plane paper normally.

$$ \begin{aligned} & L P \perp d l \\ & M P \perp d l \\ & L P=M P=\sqrt{r^2+a^2} \end{aligned} $$

Now, magnetic field at $P$ due to current element at $L$ according to Biot-Savart Law,

$$ d B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{I d l \sin 90^{\circ}}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)} $$

where, $\quad a=$ radius of circular loop

$r=$ distance of point $P$ from centre along the axis. $d B \cos \phi$ components balance each other and net magnetic field is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} B & =\oint d B \sin \phi \\ & =\oint \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi}\left[\frac{I d l}{r^2+a^2}\right] \cdot \frac{a}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}} \\ & {\left[\therefore \operatorname{In} \Delta P C M \sin \phi=\frac{a}{\sqrt{r^2+a^2}}\right] } \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I a}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} \int d l \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{I a}{\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \times 2 \pi a \end{aligned} $$

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}} $$

As the direction of the field is along $+v e \mathrm{X}$-direction, we can write

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I a^2}{2\left(r^2+a^2\right)^{3 / 2}} \hat{i} $$

$$ \begin{aligned}At \,\,its \,\,\,centre,\,\,\, & r=0 \\ & B=\frac{\mu_o I a^2}{2\left(a^2\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 a} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: A bar magnet has magnetic moment of $0.05 \mathrm{Am}^2$ which is suspended in uniform magnetic field of 0.2 T . Calculate the work done in rotating the magnet from its most stable to most unstable position in the magnetic field.
questions.solution:
For most stable state, $\theta_1=0^{\circ}$ and for most unstable state $\theta_2=180^{\circ}$

∴ Work done $=U_{\text {final }}-U_{\text {initial }}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & W=-m B \cos \left(180^{\circ}\right)-\left(-m B \cos 0^{\circ}\right) \\ & W=-m B(-1)+m B=2 m B \\ & W=2 \times 0.5 \times 0.2 \\ & W=0.20 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question: A conducting solid sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$ carries a charge $Q$. The sphere is rotating about an axis passing through its center with a uniform angular speed $\omega$. The ratio of the magnitudes of the magnetic dipole moment to the angular momentum about the same axis is given as $\alpha \frac{Q}{2 M}$. The value of $\alpha$ is ___________.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{dM}=\mathrm{dIA} \\ & \mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2=\pi(\mathrm{R} \sin \theta)^2 \\ & \mathrm{dI}=\frac{\mathrm{da}}{\mathrm{T}}=\frac{\sigma(2 \pi \mathrm{r})(\mathrm{R} \mathrm{d} \theta) \omega}{2 \pi} \\ & \mathrm{dI}=\frac{\sigma 2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \omega \sin \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta}{2 \pi}\end{aligned}$

$$ \mathrm{dI}=\sigma \mathrm{R}^2 \omega \sin \theta \mathrm{~d} \theta $$

Magnetic dipole moment :

$$ \begin{aligned} & M=\int d M=\int_0^\pi \sigma R^2 \omega \pi R^2 \sin ^3 \theta d \theta \\ & M=\sigma R^4 \omega \pi \int_0^\pi \sin ^3 \theta d \theta \quad\left(\because \int_0^\pi \sin ^3 \theta d \theta=\frac{4}{3}\right) \\ & M=\left(\frac{Q}{4 \pi R^2}\right) R^4 \omega \pi\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$

Magnetic dipole moment

$$ \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{3} $$

Angular momentum

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{L}=\left(\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2\right) \omega \\ & \frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{~L}}=\frac{\mathrm{QR}^2 \omega}{3 \times \frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2 \omega}=\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2 \mathrm{M}}\left(\frac{5}{3}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$\alpha=\frac{5}{3}=1.67$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_{23}$ be real numbers such that

$$ \left(1+\frac{2}{5} x\right)^{23}=\sum\limits_{i=0}^{23} a_i x^i $$

for every real number $x$. Let $a_r$ be the largest among the numbers $a_j$ for $0 \leq j \leq 23$. Then the value of $r$ is ____________.
questions.solution:
For $\mathrm{x}=1$

$$ \left(1+\frac{2}{5}\right)^{23}=a_0+a_1+a_2+\ldots+a_{23} $$

for numerically greatest term

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{n}+1}{1+\left|\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}\right|}=\frac{23+1}{1+\frac{5}{2}}=\frac{48}{7} \\ & \Rightarrow\left[\frac{48}{7}\right]=6=\mathrm{m} \quad \text { (where [.] greatest integer function) } \end{aligned} $$

so, $\mathrm{T}_7$ is numerical greatest term

Hence $\mathrm{r}=6$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question:
The Boolean expression

$$ \sim(p \wedge q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

is equivalent to
options:
$p \wedge q$

$\sim p \wedge q$

$p \vee \sim q$

$\sim p \vee q$
questions.solution:
Using DeMorgan's law,

$$ \sim(p \wedge q)=\sim p \vee \sim q $$

Thus, our expression becomes

$$ (\sim p \vee \sim q) \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q) $$

Rearrange and group similar terms

$$ \sim p \vee(\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)) $$

Since, $\sim q \vee(p \wedge \sim q) \vee(\sim p \wedge \sim q)$ simplifies to $\sim q \vee p$, we get

$$ \sim p \vee q $$

The Boolean expression simplifies to $\sim p \vee q$.

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question:
1. If $A, B, C$ are the angles of a $\triangle A B C$, then

$$ \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \sin 2 A & \sin C & \sin B \\ \sin C & \sin 2 B & \sin A \\ \sin B & \sin A & \sin 2 C \end{array}\right| \text { is equal to } $$
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { } \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \sin A \cos A & \sin C & \sin B \\ \sin C & 2 \sin B \cos B & \sin A \\ \sin B & \sin A & 2 \sin C \cos C \end{array}\right| \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 k a \cos A & k c & k b \\ k c & 2 k b \cos B & k a \\ k b & k a & 2 k c \cos C \end{array}\right| \\ & {\left[\because \frac{\sin A}{a}=\frac{\sin B}{b}=\frac{\sin C}{c}=k\right]} \end{aligned} $$

$$ =k^3\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 a \cos A & c & b \\ c & 2 b \cos B & a \\ b & a & 2 c \cos c \end{array}\right| $$

$$ =k^3 \left\lvert\, \begin{array}{ll} a \cos A+a \cos A & a \cos B+b \cos A \\ a \cos B+b \cos A & b \cos B+b \cos B \\ c \cos A+a \cos C & b \cos C+c \cos B \end{array}\right. $$

$$ \left.\begin{aligned} & c \cos A+a \cos C \\ & b \cos C+c \cos B \\ & c \cos C+c \cos C \end{aligned} \right\rvert\, $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left.=k^3\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \cos A & a & 0 \\ \cos B & b & 0 \\ \cos C & c & 0 \end{array}\right|| | \begin{array}{ccc} a & \cos A & 0 \\ b & \cos B & 0 \\ c & \cos C & 0 \end{array} \right\rvert\, \\ & =k^3 \times 0=0 \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Consider the matrix

$$ P = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix}. $$

Let the transpose of a matrix $X$ be denoted by $X^T$. Then the number of $3 \times 3$ invertible matrices $Q$ with integer entries, such that

$$ Q^{-1} = Q^T \quad \text{and} \quad PQ = QP, $$

is
questions.solution:
To find the number of $3 \times 3$ invertible matrices $Q$ with integer entries that satisfy both $Q^{-1} = Q^T$ and $PQ = QP$, consider the given matrix $P$:

$ P = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} $

The condition $PQ = QP$ implies:

$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} a & b & c \\ d & e & f \\ g & h & i \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} $

This results in:

$ \begin{pmatrix} 2a & 2b & 2c \\ 2d & 2e & 2f \\ 3g & 3h & 3i \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2a & 2b & 3c \\ 2d & 2e & 3f \\ 2g & 2h & 3i \end{pmatrix} $

From this, we derive that:

$ c = 0, \quad f = 0, \quad h = 0, \quad g = 0 $

Thus, the matrix $Q$ is of the form:

$ Q = \begin{pmatrix} a & b & 0 \\ c & d & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & e \end{pmatrix} $

Next, using the condition $Q^T = Q^{-1}$, we have the equation:

$ Q Q^T = I $

This results in:

$ \begin{pmatrix} a & b & 0 \\ c & d & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & i \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} a & c & 0 \\ b & d & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & i \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $

This simplifies to:

$ \begin{pmatrix} a^2 + b^2 & ac + bd & 0 \\ ac + bd & c^2 + d^2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & i^2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $

From which we conclude:

$ a^2 + b^2 = 1, \quad ac + bd = 0, \quad c^2 + d^2 = 1, \quad i^2 = 1 $

Considering all possible integer solutions for these equations:

Case I:

$(a, b) = (0, 1)$ or $(0, -1)$,

$(c, d) = (1, 0)$ or $(-1, 0)$

Case II:

$(a, b) = (1, 0)$ or $(-1, 0)$,

$(c, d) = (0, 1)$ or $(0, -1)$

For each case, $e$ can be either 1 or -1 since $i^2=1$. Therefore, $e = \pm 1$.

Combining these options, we find a total of 16 possible matrices $Q$.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let $I=\left(\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)$ and $P=\left(\begin{array}{ll}2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3\end{array}\right)$. Let $Q=\left(\begin{array}{ll}x & y \\ z & 4\end{array}\right)$ for some non-zero real numbers $x, y$, and $z$, for which there is a $2 \times 2$ matrix $R$ with all entries being non-zero real numbers, such that $Q R=R P$.

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
questions.solution:
Let us consider the matrices given:

$ I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} $

$ P = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 \end{pmatrix} $

$ Q = \begin{pmatrix} x & y \\ z & 4 \end{pmatrix} $

$ R = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} $

where $ x, y, z, a, b, c, d $ are non-zero real numbers. We aim to verify properties of matrix $ Q $ given the relation $ Q R = R P $.

Steps and Calculations

Matrix Equation Analysis:

Given $ Q R = R P $, we equate both sides:

$ \begin{pmatrix} a x + c y & b x + d y \\ a z + 4c & b z + 4d \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2a & 3b \\ 2c & 3d \end{pmatrix} $

From the matrix equality, we derive the following key equations:

$ a x + c y = 2a $

$ b x + d y = 3b $

$ a z + 4c = 2c $

$ b z + 4d = 3d $

Determinant Condition:

Comparing the equations:

Set $ a z = -2c $ and $ b z = -d $, resulting in $ \frac{a}{b} = \frac{2c}{d} $.

The requirement $ |R| \neq 0 $ implies $ |P| = |Q| $. So, equating determinants gives us:

$ |P| = |Q| \Rightarrow 6 = 4x - yz \quad \text{(Equation 1)} $

Condition for Non-Trivial Solutions:

From:

$ a(x - z) + c y = 0 $

$ a z + 2c = 0 $

implies a non-trivial solution matrix must have:

$ \left|\begin{array}{cc} x-2 & y \\ z & 2 \end{array}\right| = 0 \Rightarrow 2x - yz = 4 \quad \text{(Equation 2)} $

Solve for $ x $, $ yz $:

Solving Equations 1 and 2:

$ 4x - yz = 6 $

$ 2x - yz = 4 $

Subtract the second from the first:

$ (4x - yz) - (2x - yz) = 6 - 4 \Rightarrow 2x = 2 \Rightarrow x = 1 $

Plugging $ x = 1 $ back, we find:

$ 4(1) - yz = 6 \Rightarrow 4 - yz = 6 \Rightarrow yz = -2 $

Verify Determinants:

For $ Q - 2I $:

$ |Q - 2I| = \left|\begin{array}{cc} x-2 & y \\ z & 2 \end{array}\right| = 2x - yz = 0 $

For $ Q - 6I $:

$ |Q - 6I| = \left|\begin{array}{cc} x-6 & y \\ z & -2 \end{array}\right| = -2x + 12 - yz = 12 $

For $ Q - 3I $:

$ |Q - 3I| = \left|\begin{array}{cc} x-3 & y \\ z & 1 \end{array}\right| = x - 3 - yz = 0 $

Therefore, using the derived values, the statements for the determinants align correctly. The findings are:

$ |Q - 2I| = 0 $

$ |Q - 6I| = 12 $

$ yz = -2 $

These calculations validate the conditions for matrix $ Q $ concerning the predefined matrix properties.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question:
If

 $\begin{bmatrix} x & 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 45 \end{bmatrix}$

then which one of the following is a value of x?
questions.solution:
1. Compute the product $M\mathbf{v}$, where

    $M = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 2 & 3\\4 &5 &6\\7 &8 &9\end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{v}=\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\\x\end{bmatrix}$.

    $ M\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot1 + 2\cdot1 + 3\cdot x\\ 4\cdot1 + 5\cdot1 + 6\cdot x\\ 7\cdot1 + 8\cdot1 + 9\cdot x \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3x + 3\\ 6x + 9\\ 9x + 15 \end{bmatrix}. $

  2. Form the scalar

    $\begin{bmatrix}x & 1 & 1\end{bmatrix}(M\mathbf{v})$:

    $ x(3x+3) + 1(6x+9) + 1(9x+15) = 3x^2 + 3x + 6x + 9 + 9x + 15 = 3x^2 + 18x + 24. $

  3. Set this equal to 45 and solve for $x$:

    $ 3x^2 + 18x + 24 = 45 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 3x^2 + 18x - 21 = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad x^2 + 6x - 7 = 0. $

    Solving,

    $ x = \frac{-6 \pm \sqrt{36 + 28}}{2} = \frac{-6 \pm 8}{2} = 1 \text{ or } -7. $

  4. Among the given options, the value of $x$ is

    $\boxed{1}$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question:
If

 $A = \begin{bmatrix} y & z & x \\ z & x & y \\ x & y & z \end{bmatrix} $

where x,y,z are integers, is an orthogonal matrix, then what is the value of x2+y2+z2?
questions.solution:
1. Orthogonality ⇒ AᵀA = I ⇒ columns of A are orthonormal.

    Let

    C₁ = (y, z, x)ᵀ, C₂ = (z, x, y)ᵀ, C₃ = (x, y, z)ᵀ.

  2. Norm of C₁:

    $$C₁\cdot C₁=y^2+z^2+x^2=1.$$

  3. Orthogonality of C₁ and C₂:

    $$C₁\cdot C₂=yz+zx+xy=0.$$

  4. From step 2,

    $$x^2+y^2+z^2=1.$$

Answer: 1.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If $f(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{bmatrix}$ then what is (f(π))2 equal to?
questions.solution:
Given:

$$ f(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ -\sin \theta & \cos \theta \end{bmatrix} $$

We are asked to find $\left(f(\pi)\right)^2$.

Step 1: Find $f(\pi)$

Substitute $\theta = \pi$:

  • $\cos \pi = -1$

  • $\sin \pi = 0$

Therefore,

$$ f(\pi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos \pi & \sin \pi \\ -\sin \pi & \cos \pi \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} $$

Step 2: Compute $\left(f(\pi)\right)^2$

Multiply $f(\pi)$ by itself:

$ \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \times \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (-1)\times(-1) + 0\times0 & (-1)\times0 + 0\times(-1) \\ 0\times(-1) + (-1)\times0 & 0\times0 + (-1)\times(-1) \end{bmatrix} $

$ = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

Final Answer:

Option D:

$ \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If $A=\begin{bmatrix}1&2&2 \\ 2&1&2\\2&2&1\end{bmatrix}$ then what is A2−4A equal to?
options: −5I3
−I3
I3
5I3
questions.solution:
Given:

$$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$

We are required to find $A^2 - 4A$.

Step 1: Find $A^2$

Multiply $A$ by itself:

$$ A^2 = A \times A $$

Calculate each entry:

  • First row:

  • First column: $1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 2 = 1 + 4 + 4 = 9$

  • Second column: $1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8$

  • Third column: $1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8$

  • Second row:

  • First column: $2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8$

  • Second column: $2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 4 + 1 + 4 = 9$

  • Third column: $2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$

  • Third row:

  • First column: $2 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 2 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8$

  • Second column: $2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8$

  • Third column: $2 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 = 4 + 4 + 1 = 9$

So,

$$ A^2 = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix} $$

Step 2: Find $4A$

Multiply each entry of $A$ by $4$:

$$ 4A = 4 \times \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 4 \end{bmatrix} $$

Step 3: Calculate $A^2 - 4A$

Subtract $4A$ from $A^2$:

$$ A^2 - 4A = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9-4 & 8-8 & 8-8 \\ 8-8 & 9-4 & 8-8 \\ 8-8 & 8-8 & 9-4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$

This is $5I_3$ where $I_3$ is the $3 \times 3$ identity matrix.

Final Answer:

Option D:

$ 5I_3 $

is the correct answer.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question:
If $A=\begin{bmatrix}x&y&z\\y&z&x\\z&x&y\end{bmatrix}$

where x,y,z are integers, is an orthogonal matrix, then what is A2 equal to?
questions.solution:
Given:

$$ A = \begin{bmatrix} x & y & z \\ y & z & x \\ z & x & y \end{bmatrix} $$

We are told $A$ is an orthogonal matrix.

Step 1: Property of an Orthogonal Matrix

If $A$ is orthogonal,

$$ A^T A = I $$

or equivalently,

$$ A^T = A^{-1} $$

Step 2: Calculate $A^2$

By the property of inverse:

$$ A^T = A^{-1} \implies A A^T = I $$

But we need $A^2 = ?$

If $A^T = A^{-1}$:

$$ A^2 = A \cdot A $$

Let us try to determine the form of $A$ using its pattern.

Step 3: Analyze Structure Using Summation

Let the sum of all elements in any row be $x + y + z$.

If $A$ is orthogonal and all entries are integers, let us check what values $x$, $y$, and $z$ can take.

Orthogonality: Rows are mutually perpendicular (dot product = 0) and unit vectors (length = 1)

Let us check the diagonal elements of $A A^T$:

  • Row 1: $x, y, z$

  • Row 2: $y, z, x$

  • Row 3: $z, x, y$

Product of Row 1 with itself:

$$ x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1 $$

Dot product Row 1 and Row 2:

$$ x \cdot y + y \cdot z + z \cdot x = 0 $$

Similarly, Row 2 and Row 3:

$$ y \cdot z + z \cdot x + x \cdot y = 0 $$

So we get:

  1. $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$

  2. $x y + y z + z x = 0$

Integers $x, y, z$ satisfying this are either all $0$ except one is $\pm 1$ or permutations.

Let’s try $x = 1, y = 1, z = -1$:

  • $x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \neq 1$

Try $x = 1, y = 0, z = 0$:

  • $1^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 = 1$

  • $1\times0 + 0\times0 + 0\times1 = 0$

So $(1,0,0)$ works. Similarly, $(0,1,0)$ and $(0,0,1)$.

But for such a $A$:

  • With $x = 1, y = 0, z = 0$,

$$ A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

Let's square this matrix:

$$ A^2 = A \cdot A = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix} $$

Calculate row-wise:

  • Row 1: $[1, 0, 0] \cdot$ columns = $[1, 0, 0]$

  • Row 2: $[0, 0, 1] \cdot$ columns = $[0, 1, 0]$

  • Row 3: $[0, 1, 0] \cdot$ columns = $[0, 0, 1]$

So:

$$ A^2 = I $$

Step 4: Conclude

Thus,

$$ A^2 = I $$

Final Answer:

Option B: Identity Matrix

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following in respect of non-singular matrices A and B:

I. $(AB) ^ {- 1} = A ^ {- 1} B ^ {- 1}$

II. $ (BA)(AB)^{-1}=I$ = I where I is the identity matrix

III. $ (AB)^{T}=A^{T}B^{T} $

How many of the above are correct?
questions.solution:
Let us check each statement one by one.

Since $A$ and $B$ are non-singular matrices, both $A^{-1}$ and $B^{-1}$ exist.

Statement I

$$ (AB)^{-1} = A^{-1}B^{-1} $$

This is not correct.

From the property of inverse of a product, we know:

$$ (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1} $$

The order gets reversed.

So, Statement I is false.

Statement II

$$ (BA)(AB)^{-1} = I $$

Now use the correct inverse formula:

$$ (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1} $$

Substitute this:

$$ (BA)(AB)^{-1} = (BA)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) $$

Using associativity,

$$ = B(AB^{-1})A^{-1} $$

But in general, matrices do not commute, so we cannot simplify this to $I$.

Let us group properly:

$$ (BA)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = B(AA^{-1}) $$

This grouping is not valid directly because matrix multiplication is associative, not commutative.

Correctly,

$$ (BA)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = B(AB^{-1})A^{-1} $$

This is not necessarily equal to $I$.

So, Statement II is false in general.

Statement III

$$ (AB)^T = A^T B^T $$

This is also not correct.

The transpose of a product follows the rule:

$$ (AB)^T = B^T A^T $$

Again, the order gets reversed.

So, Statement III is false.

Final Answer

None of the three statements are correct.

So, the correct option is:

$$ \boxed{\text{Option 1: None}} $$

questions.title: BITSAT 2025
questions.question: The acceleration versus time graph of a particle moving along a straight line is shown in the figure. Draw the respective velocity time graph. (Assuming at $t=0, v=0$ )
questions.solution:
From $t=0$ to $t=2 \mathrm{~s}, a=+2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

$$ \therefore \quad v=a t=2 t $$

or $v-t$ graph is a straight line passing through origin with slope $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$.

At the end of 2 s ,

$$ v=2 \times 2=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} $$

From $t=2$ to $4 \mathrm{~s}, a=0$

Hence, $v=4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ will remain constant.

From $t=4 \mathrm{~s}$ to $6 \mathrm{~s}, a=-4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

Hence, $v=u-a t=4-4 t$ (with $t=0$ at 4 s )

$v=0$ at $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$ or at 5 s from origin.

At the end of $6 \mathrm{~s}($ or $t=2 \mathrm{~s}), v=-4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$.

$$ \text { Corresponding } v-t \text { graph is as shown below } $$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question: The heating of NH4NO2 at 60–70 °C and NH4NO3 at 200–250 °C is associated with the formation of nitrogen containing compounds X and Y, respectively. X and Y, respectively, are
questions.solution:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text { heat, } 60-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} X \\ & \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text { heat, } 200-250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} Y \end{aligned} $$
$\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_2$ on heating at $60-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ gives decomposition reaction.
The reactant ammonium nitrite is unstable and readily decomposes into nitrogen gas $\left(\mathrm{N}_2\right)$ and water $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ ). The decomposition is faster at higher temperatures, and in concentrated aqueous solutions.
The chemical equation for the heating of $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_2$ can be written as
$$ \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_2 \frac{\Delta}{6 \mathrm{O}-70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{~N}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$
So, the product $x$ is $N_2$
Heating ammonium nitrate $\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3\right)$ at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ - $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ leads to decomposition reaction.
The product of heating $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$ are nitrous oxide ( $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}$ ) and water ( $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ ). The reaction is exothermic and the salt leaves no residue on heating.
The chemical reaction can be written as
$$ \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3 \xrightarrow[200-250 \mathrm{C}]{\Delta} \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} $$
So, the product $y$ is $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Answer: Option (A) $\mathrm{N}_2$ and $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 2 Online
questions.question:
Let $S$ denote the locus of the mid-points of those chords of the parabola $y^2=x$, such that the area of the region enclosed between the parabola and the chord is $\frac{4}{3}$. Let $\mathcal{R}$ denote the region lying in the first quadrant, enclosed by the parabola $y^2=x$, the curve $S$, and the lines $x=1$ and $x=4$.

Then which of the following statements is (are) TRUE?
questions.solution:
$$ y^2=x $$

chord with given mid point

$$ \begin{aligned} & T=S_1 \\ & y k-\frac{x+h}{2}=k^2-h \\ & 2 k y-x-h=2 k^2-2 h \end{aligned} $$

Now,

$\begin{aligned} & \left.A=\int_{y_1}^{y_2}\left(2 k y-2 k^2+h\right)-y^2\right) d y=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \left(k y^2+\left(h-2 k^2\right) y-\frac{y^3}{3}\right)_{y_1}^{y_2}=\frac{4}{3} \\ & k\left(y_2^2-y_1^2\right)+\left(h-2 k^2\right)\left(y_2-y_1\right)-\frac{1}{3}\left(y_2^3-y_1^3\right)=\frac{4}{3} \\ & \left(y_2-y_1\right)\left[k-2 k+h-2 k^2-\frac{1}{3}\left(4 k^2-2 k^2+h\right)\right]=\frac{4}{3} \\ & 2\left(h-k^2\right)^{1 / 2}\left[2 h-2 k^2\right]=4 \\ & \left(h-k^2\right)^{3 / 2}=1 \\ & x-y^2=1 \Rightarrow y^2=x-1\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & A=\int_1^4(\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{x-1}) d x \\ & =\frac{2}{3}\left[x^{3 / 2}-(x-1)^{3 / 2}\right]_1^4=\frac{2}{3}[8-3 \sqrt{3}-1] \\ & =\frac{14}{3}-2 \sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$

Solving chord and parabola

$$ \begin{aligned} & y k-\frac{y+h}{2}=k^2-h \\ & 2 k y-y^2-h=2 k^2-2 h \\ & y^2-2 k y+2 k^2-h=0 \\ & y_1+y_2=2 k \\ & y_1 y_2=2 k^2-h \\ & y_2-y_1=\sqrt{4 k^2-8 k^2+4 x} \\ & =2 \sqrt{h-k^2} \end{aligned} $$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: A tangent to the parabola y<sup>2</sup> = 4x is inclined at an angle 45° deg with the positive direction of x-axis. What is the point of contact of the tangent and the parabola?
questions.solution:
1. For the parabola $y^2=4x$, we have $4a=4\implies a=1$.

  2. Slope of the tangent $m=\tan45^\circ=1$.

  3. Equation of tangent in slope–intercept form:

    $ y=mx+\frac{a}{m} =1\cdot x+\frac{1}{1} =x+1. $

  4. Point of contact $(x,y)$ lies on both $y^2=4x$ and $y=x+1$. Substitute $y=x+1$ into $y^2=4x$:

    $ (x+1)^2=4x \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2+2x+1-4x=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x^2-2x+1=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; (x-1)^2=0 \;\Longrightarrow\; x=1. $

    Then $y=1+1=2$.

  5. Point of contact is $\boxed{(1,2)}$.

Option D.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question:
Consider the following statements in respect of the equation $x ^ 2 + 3y = 0$

I. The equation represents the equation to parabola that opens upwards.

II. The axis of the parabola is x = 0

III. The equation of the latus rectum is 4y - 3 = 0

How many of the statements given above are correct?
questions.solution:
Write the given equation in standard parabola form.

Given:

$$x^2 + 3y = 0$$

So,

$$x^2 = -3y$$

Now compare with the standard form:

$$x^2 = 4ay$$

Here,

$$4a = -3 \Rightarrow a = -\frac{3}{4}$$

This is a parabola with vertex at $(0,0)$ and axis along the $y$-axis.

Now check each statement:

I. The equation represents a parabola that opens upwards.

Since

$$x^2 = -3y$$

the coefficient of $y$ is negative, so the parabola opens downwards, not upwards.

So, Statement I is false.

II. The axis of the parabola is $x=0$

For a parabola of the form

$$x^2 = 4ay$$

the axis is the $y$-axis, whose equation is

$$x=0$$

So, Statement II is true.

III. The equation of the latus rectum is $4y-3=0$

For

$$x^2 = 4ay$$

the latus rectum is the line

$$y=a$$

Here,

$$a=-\frac{3}{4}$$

So the latus rectum is

$$y=-\frac{3}{4}$$

or

$$4y+3=0$$

But given statement says

$$4y-3=0$$

which is wrong.

So, Statement III is false.

Hence, only one statement is correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is:

$$\boxed{\text{One}}$$

So, Option 2 is correct.

questions.question: The ionic radii of $\mathrm{N}^{3-}, \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$follow the trend
questions.solution:
The correct order is $\mathrm{N}^{3-}>\mathrm{O}^{2-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}$.

All are isoelectronic (10 electrons), and nuclear charge increases from $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$.

Greater nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, reducing size.

Hence, size order: $\mathrm{N}^{3-}>\mathrm{O}^{2-}>\mathrm{F}^{-}$.

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2025 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
Let the set of all relations $R$ on the set $\{a, b, c, d, e, f\}$, such that $R$ is reflexive and symmetric, and $R$ contains exactly $10$ elements, be denoted by $\mathcal{S}$.

Then the number of elements in $\mathcal{S}$ is ________________.
questions.solution:
Problem restated: Let $R$ be a relation on $X = \{a,b,c,d,e,f\}$.

We want $R$ to be reflexive and symmetric, and contain exactly 10 elements.

Let $\mathcal{S} = \{R : R \text{ satisfies this}\}$. Find $|\mathcal{S}|$.

Step 1. Size of the universe

$|X| = 6$.

So $X \times X$ has $36$ ordered pairs.

Step 2. Reflexivity

Reflexive means we must include all $(x,x)$ pairs.

So there are 6 forced elements.

So any reflexive relation on $X$ automatically contains:

$ \{(a,a),(b,b),(c,c),(d,d),(e,e),(f,f)\}. $

So our reflexive relation starts with 6 elements.

Step 3. Symmetry

For off-diagonal elements:

If we include $(x,y)$ (for $x \neq y$), we must also include $(y,x)$.

So these off-diagonal pairs come in symmetric pairs (each of size 2).

There are $\binom{6}{2} = 15$ distinct unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$, each would correspond to either including both $(x,y)$ and $(y,x)$, or excluding both. So think of them as independent choices.

Step 4. Required size

Total size should be 10.

We already have 6 diagonal pairs.

So we need 4 more pairs.

But each symmetric pair contributes 2 elements.

So to get 4 additional, we must include exactly 2 symmetric pairs.

Step 5. Counting

Number of unordered pairs = 15.

We must choose 2 of them to include.

So total = $\binom{15}{2} = 105$.

Final Answer:

$ |\mathcal{S}| = \boxed{105} $

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: How many 7-letter words (with or without meaning) can be constructed using all the letters of the word CAPITAL so that all the consonants come together in each word?
questions.solution:
Step 1: Letters in the word CAPITAL

Word = C, A, P, I, T, A, L

  • Total letters = 7

  • Vowels = A, I, A (3 vowels, with A repeated twice)

  • Consonants = C, P, T, L (4 consonants)

Step 2: Condition that all consonants are together

When consonants are together, treat them as one single block.

  • This block = {C, P, T, L}

  • So now the arrangement is between: [one consonant block] + 3 vowels (A, I, A)

Thus, total units to arrange = 1 (block) + 3 (vowels) = 4 units.

(The vowels contain a repetition of A.)

Step 3: Arrangement of the 4 units

Number of ways to arrange =

$ \frac{4!}{2!} = 12 $

(Dividing by $2!$ because of the two identical A’s.)

Step 4: Arrangement inside the consonant block

There are 4 consonants (C, P, T, L), all different.

Ways to arrange them = $4! = 24$

Step 5: Total Arrangements

$ \text{Total arrangements} = 12 \times 24 = 288 $

Final Answer:

Option C: 288

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: How many sides are there in a polygon that has 20 diagonals?
questions.solution:
1. Formula for number of diagonals in an n-sided polygon:

    From each vertex, you can draw diagonals to the other $(n - 3)$ non-adjacent vertices.

    Total diagonals $= \dfrac{n(n - 3)}{2}$.

  2. Set this equal to 20:

    $$\frac{n(n - 3)}{2} = 20$$

    Multiply both sides by 2:

    $$n(n - 3) = 40$$

    Expand:

    $$n^2 - 3n - 40 = 0$$

  3. Solve the quadratic equation $n^2 - 3n - 40 = 0$.

    Discriminant $D = (-3)^2 - 4 \times 1 \times (-40) = 9 + 160 = 169$.

    $$n = \frac{3 \pm \sqrt{169}}{2} = \frac{3 \pm 13}{2}$$

    Positive root:

    $$n = \frac{3 + 13}{2} = \frac{16}{2} = 8$$

  4. Therefore, the polygon has 8 sides.

Answer: Option C (8)

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: In how many ways can the letters of the word DELHI be arranged, keeping the positions of vowels and consonants unchanged?
questions.solution:
1. Vowels in “DELHI”: E, I

    Consonants in “DELHI”: D, L, H

  2. Positions in the word DELHI:

    • 1 → consonant

    • 2 → vowel

    • 3 → consonant

    • 4 → consonant

    • 5 → vowel

  3. Number of ways to arrange the 3 consonants (D, L, H) in the 3 consonant‐positions:

    $$3! \;=\; 6$$

  4. Number of ways to arrange the 2 vowels (E, I) in the 2 vowel‐positions:

    $$2! \;=\; 2$$

  5. Total number of arrangements keeping vowel/consonant positions unchanged:

    $$3!\times 2! \;=\; 6\times 2 \;=\; 12$$

Answer: 12 (Option C)

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the number of positive integer solutions of x+y+z=5?
questions.solution:
1. For positive integers $x,y,z$, set

    $ x = a+1,\quad y = b+1,\quad z = c+1, $

    where $a,b,c\ge0$.

  2. Then

    $ x+y+z = 5 \;\Longrightarrow\; (a+1)+(b+1)+(c+1)=5 \;\Longrightarrow\; a+b+c = 5-3 = 2. $

  3. Number of non-negative integer solutions of $a+b+c=2$ is by stars and bars

    $ \binom{2+3-1}{3-1} = \binom{4}{2} = 6. $

Answer: 6.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: If the number of selections of r as well as (n+r) things from 5n different things are equal, then what is the value of r?
questions.solution:
1. Given

    $$\binom{5n}{r} = \binom{5n}{n+r}.$$

  2. Recall the identity

    $$\binom{N}{k} = \binom{N}{N-k}.$$

    Here $N=5n$.

  3. Therefore equality holds if either

    a) $$r = 5n - (n+r)\quad\text{or}\quad b)\;r = n+r.$$

  4. Case (b): $r = n+r\implies n=0$, not acceptable for general $n>0$.

  5. Case (a):

    $$r = 5n - n - r = 4n - r\;\implies\;2r = 4n\;\implies\;r = 2n.$$

Answer: 2n (Option B).

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 13 April 2025
questions.question: What is the number of selections of at most 3 things from 6 different things?
questions.solution:
1. “At most 3 things” means you can select 0, 1, 2 or 3 items.

  2. Number of ways to choose $k$ items from 6 is given by the binomial coefficient $\displaystyle \binom{6}{k}$.

  3. Total number of selections

    $$\sum_{k=0}^{3}\binom{6}{k} = \binom{6}{0} + \binom{6}{1} + \binom{6}{2} + \binom{6}{3}$$

  4. Compute each term:

    $\displaystyle \binom{6}{0}=1,\quad \binom{6}{1}=6,\quad \binom{6}{2}=15,\quad \binom{6}{3}=20$

  5. Sum them:

    $$1 + 6 + 15 + 20 = 42$$

Answer: Option D.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: How many 4-digit numbers that are divisible by 4 can be formed using the digits 1, 2, 3 and 4 (repetition of digits is not allowed)?
questions.solution:
A number is divisible by $4$ if its last two digits form a number divisible by $4$.

We have to form $4$-digit numbers using $1,2,3,4$ without repetition.

So first, let us find all possible last two-digit endings using these digits that are divisible by $4$.

Possible two-digit numbers from $1,2,3,4$ without repetition:

$$12,\ 13,\ 14,\ 21,\ 23,\ 24,\ 31,\ 32,\ 34,\ 41,\ 42,\ 43$$

Among these, the ones divisible by $4$ are:

$$12,\ 24,\ 32$$

So there are $3$ choices for the last two digits.

Now for each such choice, the remaining $2$ digits can be arranged in the first two places in:

$$2! = 2$$

ways.

Hence total numbers:

$$3 \times 2 = 6$$

Therefore, the required number of $4$-digit numbers is

$$\boxed{6}$$

So the correct option is:

$$\boxed{\text{Option 2}}$$

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: How many triangles can be formed by joining these points?
questions.solution:
To form a triangle, we must choose any $3$ points such that they are not collinear.

Step 1: Total ways of choosing 3 points from 8 points

$$ {}^8C_3 = \frac{8 \cdot 7 \cdot 6}{3 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = 56 $$

Step 2: Subtract the sets of 3 collinear points

It is given that $4$ points are collinear.

Any choice of $3$ points from these $4$ points will not form a triangle.

Number of such choices:

$$ {}^4C_3 = 4 $$

Step 3: Required number of triangles

$$ 56 - 4 = 52 $$

So, the number of triangles formed is

$$ \boxed{52} $$

Hence, the correct option is Option 4.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14th September 2025
questions.question: How many quadrilaterals can be formed by joining these points?
questions.solution:
To form a quadrilateral, we need to choose $4$ points such that no three of them are collinear in a way that makes the figure degenerate.

We are given:

  • Total points $= 8$

  • Out of these, $4$ points are collinear

First, find the total number of ways to choose any $4$ points from $8$:

$$ \binom{8}{4} = 70 $$

But among these, the sets of $4$ points that all lie on the same straight line cannot form a quadrilateral.

Since exactly $4$ points are collinear, the number of such invalid selections is:

$$ \binom{4}{4} = 1 $$

So, required number of quadrilaterals is:

$$ 70 - 1 = 69 $$

Hence, the correct answer is:

$$ \boxed{69} $$

So, Option 2 is correct.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which one of the following statements is correct ?
options: A neutron is formed by combination of an electron and a proton. Therefore, it is neutral
The mass of an electron is about 1/2000 times that of a proton
An isotope of cobalt is used in the treatment of goiter
J. J. Thomson proposed that the nucleus of an atom contains only neutrons
questions.solution:
Option 2 is correct.

Explanation:

The mass of an electron is

$$m_e \approx 9.11\times10^{-31}\,\text{kg},$$

while that of a proton is

$$m_p \approx 1.67\times10^{-27}\,\text{kg}.$$

Their ratio is

$$\frac{m_e}{m_p}\approx\frac{1}{1836}\approx\frac{1}{2000}.$$

Why the others are wrong:

Option 1 describes an outdated idea; neutrons aren’t simply proton+electron combinations.

Option 3 mixes up treatments: radioiodine isotopes help treat goiter, not cobalt.

Option 4 is incorrect—J. J. Thomson’s model didn’t place neutrons in the nucleus (neutrons were discovered later by Chadwick).

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which one of the following particles in the nucleus of an atom was discovered by J. Chadwick?
questions.solution:
Looking at the history of particle discoveries:

The electron was discovered by J.J. Thomson.

The proton was identified by Ernest Rutherford.

The positron was discovered by Carl Anderson.

The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick.

And among the particles listed, the electron is found outside the nucleus, while protons and neutrons are found inside the nucleus. The positron is an antiparticle and not a standard part of the nucleus.

So, the particle in the nucleus discovered by J. Chadwick is the Neutron. That's option 4!

Chadwick's discovery in 1932 was super important for understanding the atom. He found this neutral particle that had a mass very close to that of a proton, which helped explain the mass of atoms that couldn't just be accounted for by protons alone.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question: Which one of the following findings is NOT observed in Rutherford's Nuclear Model of Atom?
options: There is a neutral centre in an atom called nucleus
Nearly all the mass of an atom resides in the nucleus
The electrons revolve around the nucleus in a circular path
The size of a nucleus is very small as compared to the size of atom
questions.solution:
The statement NOT supported by Rutherford’s model is:

Option 1: “There is a neutral centre in an atom called nucleus.”

Rutherford’s gold-foil experiment showed the nucleus is extremely small, contains nearly all the atom’s mass and carries a positive charge. Electrons do revolve around it in the surrounding empty space.

questions.title: NDA General Ability 13 April 2025
questions.question:
Match List I with List II and select the answer using the code given below the Lists:

code:

2.They travel at more or less at a constant rate
Select the answer using the code given below:
